When a bucket becomes noncompliant, Config can invoke a Systems Manager Automation remediation action to remove the offending public permissions automatically, satisfying the requirement to detect and fix public access with the least operational effort.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Security Hub aggregates and prioritizes security findings across services but does not natively perform automatic remediation of S3 permissions on its own. Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor only reports on bucket permissions as a best-practice check and provides no automatic remediation capability. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses software vulnerabilities and network reachability for compute workloads such as EC2 and ECR images; it does not evaluate or remediate S3 bucket access policies. Only AWS Config combines continuous detection with automated remediation for this scenario.

153.text:
Option C is correct because an egress-only internet gateway is the IPv6 equivalent of a NAT gateway: it allows instances in a VPC to initiate outbound IPv6 connections to the internet while preventing the internet from initiating inbound connections to those instances. Pointing all IPv6 traffic (::/0) in the subnet's route table to the egress-only internet gateway gives the instances internet access for updates and outbound calls while keeping them unreachable from the internet, exactly as required.

Option A is incorrect because NAT gateways operate on IPv4 only and cannot route IPv6 traffic. Option B is incorrect because an internet gateway provides bidirectional IPv6 connectivity, which would make the instances reachable from the internet, violating the requirement. Option D is incorrect because it combines an internet gateway (which exposes instances to inbound IPv6 traffic) with a NAT gateway for IPv4 that is irrelevant in IPv6-only subnets; this neither preserves the inbound restriction nor matches the IPv6-only design.

154.text:
Option A is correct because the traffic captured by a VPC flow log is fixed when the flow log is created and cannot be changed afterward. The filter setting determines whether the log records accepted traffic, rejected traffic, or all traffic. Since rejected traffic is missing, the existing flow log was created to capture only accepted traffic, so the administrator must create a new flow log with the filter set to capture all traffic to record both accepted and rejected flows.

Option C is incorrect because the traffic type (filter) of an existing flow log is immutable; you cannot edit an existing flow log to change what traffic it captures. Options B and D are incorrect because the record format controls which fields appear in each log entry, not whether rejected traffic is captured at all; changing or customizing fields does not cause rejected traffic to be logged. The only way to include rejected traffic is to create a new flow log with the appropriate filter setting.

155.text:
Options B and C are correct because monitoring traffic between individual ECS tasks requires each task to have its own elastic network interface, which is achieved by specifying the awsvpc network mode in the task definition. The awsvpc mode gives every task a dedicated ENI with its own private IP, isolating its traffic. With per-task ENIs in place, enabling VPC Flow Logs on those network interfaces captures the IP traffic flowing to and from each task, providing the visibility needed to monitor task-to-task communication.

Option A is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs collects application and container log output, not network flow records, and is not configured on an ENI to capture traffic flows. Option D is incorrect because the bridge network mode places tasks behind the shared Docker bridge on the host, so tasks do not get individual ENIs and per-task traffic cannot be isolated. Option E is incorrect because the host network mode binds tasks directly to the host's network interface, again preventing per-task ENI-level flow capture. Only awsvpc plus VPC Flow Logs meets the requirement.

156.text:
Option C is correct because CloudFormation nested stacks let you factor out commonly repeated components into dedicated, reusable child templates, each with its own parameters and conditions. A parent template references these child templates through AWS::CloudFormation::Stack resources, so the shared components are defined once and reused across deployments. This directly addresses the duplication of identical components across multiple templates while keeping each component template self-contained.

Option A is incorrect because a change set previews the modifications a stack update will make; it does not modularize or reuse common components. Option B is incorrect because CloudFormation macros perform template processing and transformations using Lambda, which is far more complex than needed and is not the standard mechanism for reusing common resource definitions. Option D is incorrect because StackSets deploy the same stack across multiple accounts and Regions; they address multi-account distribution, not the modular reuse of duplicated components within templates. Nested stacks are the purpose-built feature for sharing common, parameterized components.

157.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes configuration and CloudWatch utilization metrics to generate right-sizing recommendations, and it specifically supports AWS Lambda functions by recommending optimal memory size to improve performance and cost. Compute Optimizer works across accounts when integrated with AWS Organizations, and its recommendations can be exported to an S3 bucket, satisfying the multi-account requirement and the need for per-function optimal configuration guidance.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS Serverless Application Repository is a catalog for sharing and deploying serverless application templates; it provides no resource optimization recommendations. Option C is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail Insights detects unusual API activity patterns and does not produce Lambda resource-sizing recommendations, even with all Organizations features enabled. Option D is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor offers cost and performance checks for services like EC2 and idle resources but does not generate per-function optimal memory configuration recommendations for Lambda the way Compute Optimizer does. Compute Optimizer is purpose-built for this Lambda recommendation use case.

158.text:
Option A is correct because CloudWatch Logs Insights is a purpose-built, interactive query engine for log data stored in CloudWatch Logs. It lets you run a single query across multiple log groups at once and use the stats command with the count function to aggregate matching error events grouped by type. This directly produces the requested count of application errors by type across all of the Lambda log groups without exporting data or building extra infrastructure.

Option B is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs search does not provide a groupby keyword or an aggregation function for grouped counts; that capability belongs to Logs Insights queries. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Athena queries data in Amazon S3, so you would first have to export and structure the logs in S3, adding significant overhead before any query could run. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS is a relational database and does not store CloudWatch Logs data; you cannot query log groups with SQL against RDS without first ingesting the logs elsewhere.

159.text:
Option B is correct because CloudFront caches objects at edge locations for the duration of the TTL, which is set to 86,400 seconds (one day) here, so users continue to receive the old cached content until it expires. Creating an invalidation on the distribution for the updated S3 objects removes those objects from the edge caches immediately, forcing CloudFront to fetch the new versions from the origin on the next request. This makes the updated website visible to users as quickly as possible.

Option A is incorrect because adjusting the DNS CNAME TTL affects DNS resolution caching, not the content cached at CloudFront edge locations, so users would still see stale content. Option C is incorrect because creating a whole new distribution and repointing DNS is slow, disruptive, and unnecessary when an invalidation solves the problem directly. Option D is incorrect because pointing DNS straight to the S3 bucket bypasses CloudFront entirely, removing the CDN benefits and acceleration, which is not the intended fix for stale cached objects.

160.text:
Option B is correct because the CloudFormation DependsOn attribute explicitly defines a creation order dependency between resources. By adding DependsOn to the EC2 instance resource and referencing the logical name of the RDS DB instance, CloudFormation will not begin launching the EC2 instance until the RDS DB instance has been successfully created. This is the standard, declarative way to enforce that one resource is provisioned before another.

Option A is incorrect because a wait condition is used to pause for an external signal (such as configuration completion) rather than to sequence the creation of one resource before another, and it adds unnecessary complexity. Option C is incorrect because CloudFormation does not guarantee creation order based on the textual position of resources in the template; it parallelizes resource creation unless an explicit dependency is declared. Option D is incorrect because using separate templates with StackSets to coordinate ordering is far more complex than needed and StackSets is designed for multi-account, multi-Region deployment, not for ordering two resources within a single stack.

161.text:
Option A is correct because a geolocation routing policy routes traffic based on the geographic location of the user's DNS query, allowing you to direct visitors in Europe to the eu-central-1 endpoint and all other visitors to the us-east-1 endpoint. By creating a geolocation record for the European continent pointing to eu-central-1 and a default record pointing to us-east-1, Route 53 sends each user to the correct Regional website based on where they are located, exactly as required.

Option B is incorrect because geoproximity routing is based on the distance between users and resources with adjustable bias values, which is intended for shifting traffic proportionally rather than cleanly assigning a continent to a specific endpoint. Option C is incorrect because latency routing sends users to the Region with the lowest network latency, which does not guarantee that all European users reach eu-central-1 or that all others reach us-east-1. Option D is incorrect because multivalue answer routing returns multiple healthy records at random for load distribution and does not consider the user's geographic location at all.

162.text:
Option C is correct because CloudFront geo restriction is a built-in feature that lets you allow or block access to your distribution based on the country associated with the viewer's IP address. By enabling geo restriction and specifying the unauthorized countries in a blocklist (or the authorized ones in an allowlist), CloudFront denies requests from prohibited locations automatically. This requires only a configuration change on the distribution, making it the most operationally efficient solution.

Option A is incorrect because the s3:LocationConstraint condition refers to the Region in which a bucket is created, not the geographic location of the requester, so it cannot restrict viewers by country. Option B is incorrect because an origin access identity controls whether CloudFront can read from the S3 bucket; it has no concept of viewer country and cannot block users by location. Option D is incorrect because mapping authorized countries to IP ranges and generating signed URLs per IP is complex, error-prone, and high-maintenance compared to simply enabling the native geo restriction feature in CloudFront.

163.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Trusted Advisor includes a Low Utilization Amazon EC2 Instances check that identifies instances with consistently low CPU utilization. Trusted Advisor can publish check status changes to an Amazon SNS topic, and an AWS Lambda function subscribed to that topic can then act on the findings to stop or terminate the idle instances. This delivers automated monitoring of CPU utilization and remediation of underutilized instances using managed checks rather than custom metric logic.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes for malicious activity; it does not evaluate EC2 CPU utilization or generate idle-instance findings. Option B is incorrect because the AWS Health Dashboard reports on service events and scheduled maintenance, not EC2 utilization metrics, so it cannot drive idle-instance termination. Option D is incorrect because Trusted Advisor's check cannot directly target the EC2 TerminateInstances API as an EventBridge target in the manner described, and it lacks the logic to identify and act on specific idle instances, making the SNS-and-Lambda approach the workable solution.

164.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Lambda allocates CPU power proportionally to the amount of memory configured for a function. Increasing the memory setting automatically grants the function more vCPU, which speeds up CPU-intensive operations and reduces execution time. This is the standard tuning lever for performance in Lambda; there is no separate CPU configuration knob, so raising memory is the correct way to give the function more processing power.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda does not expose CPU launch options or a hyperthreading toggle; CPU is controlled solely through the memory setting. Option B is incorrect because AWS managed encryption protects environment variables and data at rest and has no measurable impact on CPU performance, so turning it off would not improve speed and would weaken security. Option D is incorrect because moving code into a Lambda layer only changes how dependencies are packaged and shared; it does not increase the compute resources available to the function and therefore would not resolve a CPU bottleneck.

165.text:
Option A is correct because newly created EBS volumes restored from a snapshot normally load their blocks lazily from Amazon S3 on first access, causing initial I/O latency until the volume is fully initialized. EBS fast snapshot restore (FSR) eliminates this by fully initializing the volume so it delivers its full provisioned performance immediately. FSR must be enabled per snapshot in the specific Availability Zone where the new volumes will be created, which here is the second Availability Zone, so the restored volumes can be validated as quickly as possible.

Option B is incorrect because enabling FSR in the current Availability Zone does not benefit volumes created in the second Availability Zone; FSR is Availability Zone specific. Options C and D are incorrect because Provisioned IOPS is a volume property selected when creating an EBS volume, not an attribute you specify on a snapshot, and even a Provisioned IOPS volume restored from a snapshot still suffers the lazy-load latency unless FSR is enabled. Only FSR guarantees immediate full performance for the validation tests.

166.text:
Option B is correct because a CloudWatch alarm using the StatusCheckFailed_System metric detects failures of the underlying host or AWS infrastructure, which is exactly the hardware failure scenario described. Adding an EC2 recover action to this alarm recovers the instance on new hardware while preserving its instance ID, private IP address, Elastic IP address, and EBS volumes. Attaching an SNS notification to the alarm and subscribing the SysOps team's email address ensures the team is emailed when the recovery is initiated.

Option A is incorrect because StatusCheckFailed_Instance reflects problems within the instance itself (such as OS-level issues) rather than the underlying hardware, so it does not match the hardware-failure trigger. Options C and D are incorrect because using an Auto Scaling group launches a new, replacement instance rather than recovering the existing one, and an Auto Scaling launch template cannot guarantee retention of the same private IP and Elastic IP from the original instance; recovery via the CloudWatch EC2 action is the only approach that preserves both addresses.

167.text:
Option C is correct because the traffic increase is predictable, occurring during the same 4-hour window every day. A scheduled scaling action in the Auto Scaling group can scale out the desired capacity shortly before the surge begins and scale back in afterward, ensuring capacity is ready exactly when needed. Because the scaling is time-based and automated, it is the most operationally efficient way to handle a known, recurring traffic pattern without manual intervention.

Option A is incorrect because adding a second load balancer with weighted routing does not add compute capacity to handle more traffic and only complicates the architecture. Option B is incorrect because running larger instance types all day to cover a 4-hour peak wastes capacity and money during the majority of the day when load is consistent. Option D is incorrect because manually adding instances requires ongoing human effort, risks under- or over-provisioning, and does not automatically scale back when the peak ends, making it far less operationally efficient than a scheduled scaling action.

168.text:
Option A is correct because CloudFormation cross-stack references are the standard, low-effort mechanism for sharing values between independent stacks. By declaring an Export name on the relevant outputs (such as VPC ID, subnet IDs, and route table IDs) in the first template, those values become available account-wide in the Region. The second template then uses the Fn::ImportValue function to import them directly, establishing the dependency without any custom code or manual copying.

Option B is incorrect because building a custom resource with a Lambda function to query the first stack adds significant development and maintenance overhead compared to native exports and imports. Option C is incorrect because a Mappings section holds static key-value data within a single template and cannot be referenced from a separate stack. Option D is incorrect because manually inputting resource names and passing them as parameters requires error-prone, hand-managed coordination between deployments, whereas exporting outputs and importing values automates the linkage with the least administrative effort.

169.text:
Option B is correct because EBS snapshots are incremental: the first snapshot stores the full 10 GiB, but each subsequent snapshot saves only the blocks that have changed since the previous snapshot. The first snapshot consumes 10 GiB. The second snapshot stores only the 4 GiB of changed blocks. The third snapshot stores only the 2 GiB of newly added data. Adding these together (10 + 4 + 2) yields 16 GiB of total storage consumed across the three snapshots.

Option A (12 GiB) is incorrect because it reflects only the final logical size of the volume, ignoring that incremental snapshots retain prior changed blocks. Option C (26 GiB) incorrectly double-counts by adding 10 + 4 + 12, treating the third snapshot as a full copy. Option D (32 GiB) incorrectly assumes every snapshot stores the full volume contents (roughly 10 + 10 + 12), which contradicts the incremental nature of EBS snapshots where unchanged blocks are referenced rather than re-stored.

170.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Organizations applies service control policies (SCPs) to organizational units (OUs), and any account placed within that OU automatically inherits the policy. Grouping the developer accounts into a single OU and attaching the SCP that denies the specific EC2 instance family is the most operationally efficient approach, because it scales across multiple accounts and any future accounts moved into the OU inherit the restriction without additional configuration.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Resource Groups organize resources for management and automation tasks within an account; they are not a target for SCPs, which apply only to the organization root, OUs, or individual accounts. Option C is incorrect because applying the SCP to each developer account individually creates repetitive manual work and does not scale, which is the opposite of operationally efficient. Option D is incorrect because attaching an SCP to the AWS Control Tower management account is ineffective and discouraged: SCPs do not restrict the management account, so the developer accounts would remain unrestricted.

171.text:
Option B is correct because Application Load Balancers include a built-in health check mechanism that you configure directly on the target group. By setting the correct Health Check Path (along with protocol, port, and thresholds), the ALB periodically sends requests to each registered EC2 instance and routes traffic only to instances that respond as healthy. This is fully managed, requires no additional infrastructure, and is the native, most operationally efficient way to define a custom health check.

Option A is incorrect because the ALB does not read health status from an S3 bucket; this is a non-supported, custom workaround that adds complexity and latency. Option C is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache is an in-memory caching service and plays no role in health checking or tracking Auto Scaling activity. Option D is incorrect because Amazon API Gateway is for creating and managing APIs, not for performing ALB target health checks, and introducing it would add unnecessary operational overhead and architectural complexity.

172.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon RDS Proxy is a fully managed database proxy that pools and shares established database connections, dramatically reducing the number of open connections to the Aurora DB cluster. By multiplexing many idle application connections over a smaller set of database connections, RDS Proxy prevents the database from exhausting its connection limit and eliminates the "Too many connections" error. The application simply points to the RDS Proxy endpoint instead of the direct cluster endpoint.

Option A is incorrect because read capacity units and write capacity units are DynamoDB concepts and do not apply to Amazon Aurora, which scales by instance class and connection management. Option C is incorrect because enhanced networking improves network throughput and packet-per-second performance but does nothing to address connection limits. Option D is incorrect because moving to a burstable instance type does not increase the maximum connection limit in a way that resolves the problem; the issue is connection pooling, not raw compute or burst capacity.

173.text:
Options B and D are correct because attaching a policy to an IAM user group lets you grant the same permissions to multiple IAM users simultaneously, so adding users to the group automatically applies the policy. Using a customer managed policy is required because, unlike AWS managed policies, customer managed policies are fully editable by the customer and support versioning—you can modify the policy and create new versions, with the ability to roll back. Together these meet the need to provide access to multiple users while allowing the policy to be changed and versioned.

Option A is incorrect because service-linked roles are predefined roles linked to specific AWS services; you cannot add IAM users to them. Option C is incorrect because AWS managed policies are created and maintained by AWS and cannot be edited or versioned by the customer. Option E is incorrect because an inline policy is embedded directly in a single principal, is not reusable across multiple users, and does not support managed versioning.

174.text:
Option D is correct because Elastic Load Balancer access logs capture detailed information about each request sent to the load balancer, including the source (client) IP address, request paths, latencies, and response codes. Since the spike was observed in the ALB's RequestCount metric, enabling and examining the ALB access logs (stored in Amazon S3) is exactly where the administrator can identify the source IP addresses of the incoming requests.

Option A is incorrect because Auto Scaling activity logs record scaling events such as instance launches and terminations, not client request source IPs. Option B is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail logs record AWS API calls and management-plane activity, not the data-plane HTTP request details like client IP addresses hitting the load balancer. Option C is incorrect because EC2 instance logs may contain application or system data, but in an ALB architecture the load balancer is the entry point that records client source IPs in its access logs; relying on individual instance logs is inconsistent and less reliable than the ALB access logs.

175.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Object Lock in compliance mode enforces write-once, read-many (WORM) protection where no user—including the root account—can delete or overwrite a locked object version until the retention period expires. Creating a new bucket with Object Lock enabled and placing the backups there with a 3-month retention period guarantees the backups cannot be deleted for at least 3 months, satisfying the requirement strictly and durably.

Option A is incorrect because an IAM deny policy is a reactive, manually managed control that can be altered or removed by an administrator, and it does not provide guaranteed immutability for the required period. Option C is incorrect because S3 Versioning and Lifecycle rules help with object recovery and transitions but do not prevent deletion; lifecycle rules can expire objects and versions can still be deleted. Option D is incorrect because, although governance mode applies a retention period, users with the s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission can override and delete the objects early, so it does not strictly guarantee the 3-month protection that compliance mode enforces.

176.text:
Option B is correct because the split-tunnel option on an AWS Client VPN endpoint controls which traffic is routed through the VPN tunnel. When split-tunnel is enabled, only traffic destined for the routes associated with the Client VPN endpoint (the VPC CIDR) is sent over the tunnel, while all other internet-bound traffic goes directly out the user's local connection. This precisely meets the compliance requirement that only VPC-destined traffic traverses the VPN tunnel.

Option A is incorrect because associating the endpoint with a private subnet that routes through a NAT gateway controls how the endpoint reaches the internet, not which client traffic enters the tunnel; by default (split-tunnel off) all client traffic would still flow through the VPN. Option C is incorrect because specifying DNS server IP addresses only configures name resolution for connected clients and does not restrict the scope of tunneled traffic. Option D is incorrect because selecting a private identity certificate handles client authentication, not traffic routing or tunnel scope.

177.text:
Option B is correct because the application runs three instances across three Availability Zones for high availability, and the Trusted Advisor check flags them as underutilized rather than redundant. Applying AWS Cost Explorer rightsizing recommendations reduces each instance to a smaller, more appropriately sized instance type, lowering cost while keeping all three instances in their respective Availability Zones. This preserves the application's availability and existing design while addressing the low-utilization cost concern.

Option A is incorrect because reducing the number of application servers would remove instances from Availability Zones and degrade the high-availability design, violating the requirement. Option C is incorrect because adding an Application Load Balancer introduces additional cost and does not address the low utilization of the existing instances. Option D is incorrect because scaling up the instance size increases cost and worsens the underutilization problem, which is the opposite of the stated goal.

178.text:
Option B is correct because the supported way to relocate an EC2 instance to a different Availability Zone is to create an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) from the existing instance, launch a new instance from that AMI into the target Availability Zone (and subnet), and then terminate the original instance. The AMI captures the instance configuration and attached volume data, allowing the workload to be recreated reliably in the new Availability Zone.

Option A is incorrect because there is no direct "copy the EC2 instance to a different Availability Zone" operation; instances are not copied across AZs in that manner. Option C is incorrect because the AWS CLI does not provide a command to simply move a running EC2 instance to another Availability Zone; the instance is bound to the subnet and AZ in which it launched. Option D is incorrect because stopping an instance and modifying its Availability Zone is not a supported action—the AZ of an instance cannot be changed by editing a setting, since it is tied to the subnet selected at launch.

179.text:
Option C is correct because CloudFormation parameters let you pass custom values into a template at stack creation or update time, so a single template can be reused across multiple environments (for example, dev, test, and production) by supplying different parameter values such as instance sizes, environment names, or CIDR ranges. This makes the template generic and reusable without editing the template body for each environment.

Option A is incorrect because EC2 user data only runs bootstrap scripts on instance launch and does not provide a mechanism for parameterizing or reusing a whole template across environments. Option B is incorrect because nested stacks are used to modularize and reuse common components across stacks, but they do not by themselves provide the per-environment value substitution that parameters provide. Option D is incorrect because stack policies control which resources can be updated to prevent accidental changes; they do not provision resources or make a template reusable across environments.

180.text:
Option A is correct because placing all of the EC2 instances in private subnets behind a single NAT gateway causes all of their outbound internet traffic to be source-translated to the NAT gateway's single Elastic IP address. Providing that one consistent public IP address to the third-party service satisfies the strict IP allow list requirement that all build uploads originate from a single IP.

Option B is incorrect because an internet gateway does not perform network address translation to a single IP; instances behind it use their own individual public IP addresses, so uploads would come from many different addresses. Option C is incorrect because an Availability Zone has no single IP address, and grouping instances by AZ does not consolidate their outbound source IPs. Option D is incorrect because a peered VPC does not present a single IP address for outbound internet traffic, and VPC peering is used for inter-VPC communication rather than for controlling the public source IP seen by an external service.

181.text:
Option A is correct because the requirement is to be alerted before throttling occurs. A CloudWatch alarm on the DynamoDB ConsumedReadCapacityUnits metric, with the threshold set close to (but below) the table's provisioned read capacity, fires while consumption is approaching the limit. Configuring the alarm to publish to the existing SNS topic notifies the operations team's subscribed email before requests are actually throttled, giving them time to react.

Option B is incorrect because while DynamoDB auto scaling can adjust capacity, the question specifically asks how to alert the team before throttling, and EventBridge notifications on scaling events occur after scaling is triggered rather than as a proactive warning of approaching limits. Option C is incorrect because DynamoDB does not publish per-request THROTTLING_EXCEPTION codes to CloudWatch Logs that you can metric-filter in this manner; throttling is monitored through CloudWatch metrics. Option D is incorrect for the same reason and because relying on application-side log pattern matching for a THROTTLING_EXCEPTION would alert only after throttling has already happened, not before.

182.text:
Option D is correct because the symptom—keys being evicted randomly regardless of whether a TTL is set—indicates an eviction policy such as allkeys-random. Changing the policy to evict the least frequently used keys (an LFU policy such as allkeys-lfu) keeps the most popular product queries in the cache while evicting genuinely cold data. This directly improves the cache hit ratio for a workload of popular queries without adding nodes or memory, so there is no cost increase.

Option A is incorrect because adding another node increases the cluster's cost, which violates the constraint. Option B is incorrect because increasing the TTL keeps entries longer but does not change the fundamentally random eviction behavior that is removing popular keys, so it does not reliably improve the hit ratio. Option C is incorrect because switching to a policy that randomly evicts keys with a TTL set still relies on random eviction and may evict frequently accessed entries, failing to optimize for the popular queries driving the hit ratio.

183.text:
Option A is correct because a cluster placement group packs instances close together on high-bandwidth, low-latency network hardware within a single Availability Zone. This arrangement is purpose-built for tightly coupled, node-to-node HPC workloads that demand minimum latency and maximum network throughput, enabling full-bisection bandwidth and the lowest possible inter-node latency.

Option B is incorrect because a cluster placement group cannot span multiple Availability Zones; clustering across AZs is not supported and would introduce cross-AZ latency that defeats the purpose. Option C is incorrect because a partition placement group spreads instances across distinct partitions (separate racks) to reduce correlated hardware failures, which increases fault tolerance but does not deliver the lowest latency or highest throughput between nodes. Option D is incorrect for the same reason and because spanning partition placement groups across multiple Availability Zones would further increase latency, the opposite of the HPC requirement.

184.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides a fully managed native Windows file system that supports the SMB protocol and Windows ACLs for file permission management. Deploying it as a Multi-AZ file system automatically provisions a standby file server in a second Availability Zone with synchronous replication and automatic failover, so data remains accessible if an Availability Zone becomes unavailable or during system maintenance. This meets all stated requirements with a single managed solution.

Option A is incorrect because a single AWS Storage Gateway file gateway is not highly available across Availability Zones and is primarily a hybrid bridge to S3, not a native SMB file server with Windows ACL management designed for AZ resilience. Option C is incorrect because placing file gateways behind an ALB does not provide the synchronous, consistent failover of a native Windows file system, and file gateways are not the appropriate solution for full SMB/Windows ACL semantics with AZ resilience. Option D is incorrect because using two Single-AZ FSx file systems with DFSR adds significant operational complexity to self-manage replication and does not provide the automatic, managed Multi-AZ failover that the built-in Multi-AZ deployment offers.

185.text:
Option C is correct because CloudFormation supports a DeletionPolicy attribute on resources, and setting it to Snapshot on the RDS instance instructs CloudFormation to automatically take a final snapshot of the database before the resource is deleted when the stack is deleted. This reliably and efficiently retains the persisted data for future use without any manual steps or custom scripts.

Option A is incorrect because writing a custom script to back up the instance every five minutes is operationally heavy, error-prone, and unnecessary given the built-in DeletionPolicy capability. Option B is incorrect because a Lambda function that must be manually invoked before each stack deletion is fragile—it depends on someone remembering to run it—whereas the Snapshot DeletionPolicy is automatic and tied directly to the deletion process. Option D is incorrect because creating and running a separate CloudFormation template for backups before deleting the stack adds complexity and manual coordination, and is not the reliable, automated approach that the native DeletionPolicy provides.

186.text:
Option B is correct because an Amazon S3 Glacier vault is designed for low-cost, long-term archival, which suits 10-year retention of audit logs. Applying a Vault Lock policy enforces write-once, read-many (WORM) controls that, once locked, cannot be changed or deleted—including by the account owner—so the logs are protected from any future edits or deletion for the duration specified. This combination meets both the long-term archival and immutability requirements.

Option A is incorrect because an EBS volume is block storage attached to instances, is comparatively expensive for 10-year archival, and KMS encryption protects confidentiality, not immutability against edits. Option C is incorrect because S3 Standard-IA with server-side encryption provides durability and encryption but does not prevent objects from being edited or deleted, so it fails the immutability requirement. Option D is incorrect because MFA (such as MFA Delete) adds a deletion safeguard but is not a true WORM control that guarantees the logs cannot be edited, and it is far less suited to enforced multi-year immutability than a Glacier Vault Lock policy.

187.text:
Option A is correct because kubectl communicates with the Amazon EKS cluster API server using connection details and credentials defined in the kubeconfig file. The administrator must generate or update this file (for example, with the aws eks update-kubeconfig command), which writes the cluster endpoint, certificate authority data, and the authentication command needed for kubectl to authenticate and issue commands against the cluster.

Option B is incorrect because kube-proxy is a networking add-on that manages network rules on cluster nodes; it does not configure the administrator's local kubectl to reach the API server. Option C is incorrect because a Fargate profile defines which pods run on AWS Fargate within the cluster and is a cluster-side configuration, not something required on the administrator's machine for kubectl connectivity. Option D is incorrect because the eks-connector.yaml file is used to register and connect external or on-premises Kubernetes clusters to the EKS console, not to enable kubectl access to a native EKS cluster.

188.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Systems Manager Session Manager relies on the SSM Agent on the instance assuming an IAM instance profile (role) that grants the necessary Systems Manager permissions, typically via the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy. Without an attached IAM role granting these permissions, the agent cannot register the instance as a managed instance, so it does not appear in the Session Manager list even though the agent is installed and running.

Option A is incorrect because Session Manager does not use SSH key pairs; it connects through the Systems Manager service, eliminating the need for key pair access. Option B is incorrect because Session Manager does not require inbound security group rules or open port 22—connectivity is established outbound through the SSM Agent, so a missing SSH security group is not the cause. Option D is incorrect because there is no requirement to manually enter an EC2 instance ID into a Session Manager configuration; managed instances are discovered automatically once the agent and proper IAM role are in place.

189.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Trusted Advisor includes a Security Groups – Specific Ports Unrestricted check that automatically identifies security groups allowing unrestricted (0.0.0.0/0) access on sensitive ports, including port 3389 (RDP). The administrator can simply review this Trusted Advisor check to obtain the list of offending security groups, meeting the requirement directly.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes logs and activity for malicious behavior; it does not produce a static list of security groups that are open on port 3389. Option B is incorrect because a service control policy is a preventive guardrail that restricts permissions across an organization; it cannot scan or report existing security group configurations. Option C is incorrect because IAM Access Analyzer evaluates resource-based policies and identifies resources shared with external entities; it does not inspect security group inbound rules for open ports.

190.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Route 53 alias records can point a zone apex (naked) domain such as company.com directly to an AWS resource like an Application Load Balancer. Alias records are a Route 53 extension that resolve to the load balancer's DNS name at the zone apex—something a standard CNAME cannot do—and they incur no additional Route 53 query charges for AWS resource targets.

Option A is incorrect because a CNAME record cannot be created at the zone apex; DNS standards prohibit a CNAME coexisting with the required SOA and NS records at the apex, so it cannot map company.com to the ALB. Option B is incorrect because an SOA record defines administrative information for the DNS zone and is not used to route traffic to a resource. Option C is incorrect because a TXT record holds arbitrary text (for example, domain verification or SPF data) and cannot direct traffic to an Application Load Balancer.

191.text:
Option C is correct because the VPC flow logs are already being published to Amazon CloudWatch Logs, and CloudWatch Logs Insights lets you run aggregation queries directly against that log data. The administrator can query the flow log fields (such as destination address and byte counts) and use sorting and limiting to identify the top five internet destinations, with no additional pipelines or data movement—making it the most operationally efficient option.

Option A is incorrect because CloudTrail Insights detects unusual API call activity in the management plane and does not contain network traffic destination data. Option B is incorrect because CloudFront access logs apply only to CloudFront distributions, which are not part of this NAT gateway architecture. Option D is incorrect because changing the flow log to publish to Amazon S3 and querying with Amazon Athena would require reconfiguring the logging destination and setting up Athena, adding unnecessary steps when the data is already in CloudWatch Logs and directly queryable with Logs Insights.

192.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Secrets Manager can securely store database credentials and natively supports automatic rotation on a schedule (such as monthly) using built-in rotation Lambda functions for RDS. RDS Proxy then pools and shares database connections, absorbing the variable and rapidly increasing client connections from the write-intensive application without overwhelming the PostgreSQL instance. RDS Proxy also integrates with Secrets Manager to retrieve the rotated credentials, so connections continue seamlessly after rotation.

Option A and Option B are incorrect because AWS KMS rotates encryption keys, not database credentials; it cannot manage and rotate the username/password used to authenticate to the database. Option B and Option D are additionally incorrect because RDS read replicas scale read traffic, but the workload here is write-intensive and the problem is handling a large, fluctuating number of client connections—read replicas neither offload writes nor provide connection pooling. Only Secrets Manager (for credential rotation) combined with RDS Proxy (for connection management) satisfies both requirements.

193.text:
Option A is correct because a Spot Fleet with an On-Demand capacity of 6 guarantees that 6 instances are always provisioned at On-Demand pricing to satisfy the hard minimum service requirement, while the remaining capacity toward the target of 10 is filled with lower-cost Spot Instances. This combination maintains uptime (the required 6 never disappear when Spot prices spike or capacity is reclaimed) while reducing cost on the extra instances, making it the most cost-effective way to preserve availability.

Option D is incorrect because a Spot Fleet with a target capacity of 6 but no On-Demand base capacity fills all 6 with Spot Instances, which can be interrupted with a two-minute warning, dropping the fleet below the required minimum and breaking the uptime guarantee. Option B keeps all instances On-Demand and meets uptime but is more expensive than mixing in Spot, so it is not the most cost-effective choice. Option C sets the minimum to 1 and maximum to 6, which violates the requirement that at least 6 instances always run to meet service requirements.

194.text:
Option B is correct because a Systems Manager managed-instance activation is the designed mechanism for registering non-EC2 servers (on-premises or other cloud) as managed instances. The administrator creates an activation that produces an activation code and ID, installs the SSM Agent on each on-premises server, and registers the servers using that code and ID. Systems Manager then issues temporary credentials to the agent, so no long-term IAM credentials are stored on the servers, satisfying the security requirement while enabling automation tasks.

Option A is incorrect because IAM roles and instance profiles can only be attached to EC2 instances, not on-premises servers, so the role cannot be applied. Option C is incorrect because an IAM policy is not a credential and cannot be "downloaded" to a server to grant it access; policies are attached to IAM principals within AWS. Option D is incorrect because creating an IAM user access key and storing it in the credentials file places long-term credentials directly on the on-premises servers, which is exactly what the requirement prohibits.

195.text:
Option C is correct because dynamic scaling (target tracking or step scaling) adjusts capacity in real time in response to a metric such as average CPU utilization. The administrator can configure a target tracking policy on the ASGAverageCPUUtilization metric with a target of 50%, and the Auto Scaling group automatically launches instances as soon as utilization rises above the threshold and removes them when demand falls. This directly meets the requirement to scale out when CPU exceeds 50%.

Option B is incorrect because scheduled scaling changes capacity at predetermined times and ignores actual CPU utilization, so it cannot react to a 50% threshold. Option D is incorrect because predictive scaling forecasts future load from historical patterns and provisions capacity ahead of time; it does not respond to a real-time CPU threshold and typically pairs with dynamic scaling rather than replacing it. Option A is incorrect because Auto Scaling does not offer a generic "scale based on events" mode for metric thresholds; reacting to CPU utilization is the function of dynamic scaling.

196.text:
Option B is correct because a CloudWatch alarm that monitors the average CPU utilization metric and is configured with an EC2 action can automatically stop an instance when the condition is met, with no custom code or polling logic to maintain. Setting the period and evaluation so the alarm triggers when average CPU stays below 10% for 60 minutes, combined with the built-in stop action, delivers the requirement using fully managed CloudWatch features, which is the most operationally efficient approach.

Option A is incorrect because a per-instance cron job requires building, deploying, and maintaining custom scripts on every instance and computing CPU utilization manually, which adds significant operational overhead. Option C is similarly inefficient because installing the CloudWatch agent and writing custom logging and shutdown logic duplicates capabilities that CloudWatch alarms already provide natively. Option D is incorrect because using Systems Manager Run Command on a schedule to gather metrics and evaluate them in custom logic is far more complex than a native alarm with an EC2 stop action.

197.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Service Catalog natively supports sharing a portfolio with other AWS accounts. The administrator shares the existing portfolio with the new account, and that account imports it, immediately gaining access to the same products and provisioning the same standardized infrastructure. Sharing avoids any duplication of definitions and keeps the portfolio centrally managed, making it the most operationally efficient way to replicate the infrastructure in the new account.

Option B is incorrect because manually recreating the entire portfolio is error-prone, time-consuming, and creates a separate copy that must be maintained independently. Option C is incorrect because writing a Lambda function around the DescribePortfolio API to rebuild the portfolio is custom, fragile code that adds operational overhead compared to native sharing. Option A is incorrect because a CloudFormation template cannot "use" an existing portfolio in another account to replicate it; portfolio sharing, not a template, is the intended cross-account mechanism.

198.text:
Option C is correct because Aurora backtracking is the feature designed to rewind an existing DB cluster to a prior point in time within a configured backtrack window (up to 72 hours) without restoring to a new cluster. The cluster is rolled back in place, satisfying the requirement that restores complete in the same production DB cluster while reaching a specific recovery point within the previous 72 hours, and it is much faster than a restore.

Option D is incorrect because point-in-time recovery in Aurora always creates a new DB cluster from backups rather than rewinding the existing cluster, which violates the requirement to restore within the same production cluster. Option A is incorrect because promoting an Aurora Replica provides failover/scaling, not the ability to roll back to a specific past recovery point. Option B is incorrect because there is no mechanism to use a Lambda function to restore an automatic backup into an existing cluster; restores from backups produce new clusters, again failing the in-place requirement.

199.text:
Option B is correct because VPC peering establishes private network connectivity between VPC1 and VPC2 so the EC2 instance can route directly to the RDS instance in the private subnet using private IP addresses. After creating the peering connection, the administrator updates route tables in both VPCs and the database security group to allow the EC2 instance's traffic, enabling the application to connect to the database privately and securely.

Option A is incorrect because route tables hold CIDR-to-target routes, not database connection strings, so entering a connection string there does nothing. Option C is incorrect because giving both VPCs the same IPv4 CIDR range causes overlapping address space, which actually prevents VPC peering and routing between them. Option D is incorrect because the DB instance resides in a private subnet and should not be exposed with a public IP address; doing so would route over the internet and is a poor security practice compared to private peering connectivity.

200.text:
Option D is correct because encryption cannot be enabled on an existing unencrypted RDS instance in place. The supported approach is to take a snapshot of the instance, copy that snapshot while selecting a KMS key to produce an encrypted copy, and then restore a new DB instance from the encrypted snapshot. The new instance is encrypted at rest, bringing the database into compliance with the audit requirement.

Option A is incorrect because the RDS console does not allow toggling encryption on an already-created unencrypted database; the encryption option is only available at creation time. Option B is incorrect because RDS manages its own storage and you cannot attach a separately created encrypted EBS volume to an RDS instance. Option C is incorrect because you cannot independently encrypt and promote the Multi-AZ standby replica; the standby shares the same storage encryption state as the primary, so this does not resolve the lack of encryption.

201.text:
Option A is correct because cross-account access to the S3 bucket is achieved with a role assumption pattern. In Account A, the Lambda execution role is granted permission to call sts:AssumeRole on a role in Account B. In Account B, a role is created with a trust policy allowing the Lambda's role to assume it and with a permissions policy granting access to the S3 bucket. At runtime the function assumes the Account B role and uses its temporary credentials to read the objects.

Option B is incorrect because a Lambda execution role in Account A cannot directly access a bucket in Account B without assuming a role or a bucket policy granting cross-account access; it simply describes the wrong trust relationship. Option C reverses the roles, placing the execution role in Account B where the function does not run, so the function cannot use it. Option D also inverts the design, granting bucket access in Account A and putting the execution role in Account B, which does not let the Account A function reach the Account B bucket.

202.text:
Option A is correct because CloudWatch anomaly detection builds a machine learning model of a metric's normal behavior from historical data and automatically establishes a band of expected values that adapts over time. This is ideal when the team does not know the application's expected usage or growth, because the administrator does not need to predict numeric thresholds; the alarm fires when the metric deviates from the learned pattern, accommodating changing baselines as the application grows.

Option C is incorrect because static thresholds require knowing expected values up front and would generate false alarms or miss issues as usage changes, which is exactly the unknown here. Option B is incorrect because composite alarms combine the states of multiple existing alarms; they do not solve the problem of not knowing what threshold to set in the first place. Option D is incorrect because treating missing data as breaching only changes how gaps in data are handled and does nothing to address unknown usage levels or growth.

203.text:
Option A is correct because CloudFront caches objects at edge locations, and when files are updated under the same object names the cached copies continue to be served until they expire. Creating a CloudFront invalidation with the path of the updated files forces CloudFront to remove those objects from its edge caches and fetch the latest versions from the S3 origin, immediately resolving the stale-content problem.

Option D is incorrect because S3 Versioning governs how object versions are stored in the bucket and has no effect on CloudFront's edge caches; disabling it would not refresh cached content and could lose version history. Option B is incorrect because signed URLs control access to restricted content and do not invalidate or refresh cached objects. Option C is incorrect because an origin access identity secures the S3 origin so it is only reachable through CloudFront; it does not control which version of content is cached or displayed.

204.text:
Option D is correct because Tag Editor in AWS Resource Groups lets the administrator query resources across all selected Regions and filter by a resource type such as AWS::EC2::Instance. It returns both tagged and untagged matching resources and displays each instance ID along with its tags, and the results can be exported. Selecting all five Regions in one query meets the requirement in a single, managed step, making it the most operationally efficient option.

Option A is incorrect because a tag-based resource group only includes resources that match specific tags, so it would exclude untagged instances and fails the requirement to identify both. Option B is incorrect because the Trusted Advisor EC2 On-Demand Instances check focuses on utilization and cost optimization, not on enumerating instance IDs with their tags across Regions. Option C is incorrect because Cost Explorer reports spend grouped by resource for billing analysis and does not list instance tags as required.

205.text:
Option B is correct because Systems Manager communicates with EC2 instances through the SSM Agent, which must have AWS credentials to call Systems Manager APIs. Attaching an IAM instance profile that includes the necessary Systems Manager permissions (for example, the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy) gives the SSM Agent the access it needs, allowing Patch Manager to register the instances and run the patch baseline during the maintenance window.

Option A is incorrect because Systems Manager uses outbound agent connectivity to its endpoints, not inbound connections, so adding an inbound security group rule does not grant Systems Manager access. Option C is incorrect because managed-instance activations are used to register non-EC2 (on-premises or hybrid) servers; native EC2 instances use an instance profile instead. Option D is incorrect because the choice between tag-based and manual instance selection is unrelated to whether Systems Manager can access the instances; access still depends on the instance profile permissions.

206.text:
Option A is correct because enabling organizational view in AWS Health aggregates Health events, including Personal Health Dashboard alerts, from all accounts in the AWS Organization into a single centralized view in the management or delegated administrator account. This automatically includes newly added accounts with no per-account setup, consolidating alerts with the least effort and removing the need to log into each account individually.

Option B is incorrect because the Personal Health Dashboard does not forward events into CloudTrail, and CloudTrail records API activity rather than aggregating health alerts. Option C is incorrect because building and maintaining a Lambda function that queries the Health API and writes to DynamoDB is custom code with significant ongoing effort compared to the built-in organizational view. Option D is similarly incorrect because directly using the Health API to populate a DynamoDB table still requires custom development and maintenance rather than the turnkey organizational view feature.

207.text:
Option B is correct because a single EC2 Auto Scaling group with a launch template can define a mixed instances policy that combines On-Demand and Spot Instances in one group, allocating a base of three On-Demand Instances for guaranteed capacity and adding Spot Instances up to the desired count. Launch templates are the current, recommended configuration mechanism and support mixed purchase options and Spot price settings, delivering high availability across Availability Zones with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because launch configurations are legacy and do not support mixed instances policies that combine On-Demand and Spot within one group. Options C and D both split the workload into two separate Auto Scaling groups, which adds unnecessary management complexity and is not required when a single group with a mixed instances policy can do the job; additionally, option C relies on legacy launch configurations. Option B's single mixed group is the simplest highly available design.

208.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Backup centrally manages backups across both Amazon EC2 and Amazon RDS resources using backup plans. The administrator creates backup plans with the required frequencies and sets a distinct retention period for each (6 days, 4 weeks, 11 months, and 7 years), then assigns resources to the plans by tag. AWS Backup automatically handles scheduling, lifecycle, and retention for both services from one place, meeting the multi-tier retention requirements with the least administrative effort.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager only manages EBS snapshots (and related EC2 resources) and does not cover RDS backups. Option D adds RDS automated backups on top of DLM, but RDS automated backup retention is limited (maximum 35 days) and cannot satisfy the monthly and 7-year requirements, while also splitting management across two tools. Option C requires building and maintaining a custom Lambda function and EventBridge rule, which is far more operational effort than the managed AWS Backup service.

209.text:
Options C and E are correct because they directly accelerate uploads over the public internet at low cost. S3 Transfer Acceleration routes uploads through the nearest CloudFront edge location and over the optimized AWS backbone to the destination bucket, reducing latency for the distant teams in Europe and Australia. Multipart uploads split large video files into parts that upload in parallel and can resume failed parts, improving throughput and resilience for large objects. Together they boost upload speed without provisioning expensive dedicated connectivity.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect requires provisioning costly dedicated physical circuits, which is far from the most cost-effective way to speed up occasional uploads. Option B is incorrect because Site-to-Site VPN traffic still traverses the public internet and adds tunnel overhead without meaningfully accelerating S3 uploads. Option D is incorrect because AWS Global Accelerator improves performance for applications behind supported endpoints like ALBs, NLBs, and EC2, but it does not front Amazon S3 bucket uploads, so it does not address this use case.

210.text:
Option C is correct because an EventBridge rule that matches the EC2 Instance State-change Notification event filtered to the running state reacts in near real time whenever an instance starts. A target Lambda function then checks the current time (to confirm it is night) and reads the instance's environment type tag to confirm it is non-production, and publishes to an SNS topic that the IT manager is subscribed to by email. This event-driven, serverless design is the most operationally efficient because nothing polls and there are no servers to manage.

Option D is incorrect because storing metadata in DynamoDB and running a custom polling application on an EC2 instance every minute adds servers and complexity. Option B similarly relies on a Lambda function polling the EC2 API every minute, which is inefficient compared to event-driven triggering. Option A is incorrect because the Health Dashboard does not emit per-instance running-state events, and adding a second throttling Lambda plus a custom SMTP client is needlessly convoluted versus EventBridge with SNS.

211.text:
Option C is correct because S3 Standard provides millisecond access and high availability for the first 90 days when the analytics team needs immediate access, with no minimum-duration or per-GB retrieval surcharges that would penalize the active period. A lifecycle rule then transitions the data to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval, whose standard retrievals complete in minutes to a few hours (well under the 5-hour requirement) at low long-term storage cost, making this the most cost-effective fit for both phases.

Option D is incorrect because S3 Glacier Deep Archive offers the lowest storage cost but standard retrievals take up to 12 hours, exceeding the under-5-hour requirement. Option A and Option B store the active-phase data in infrequent access tiers; with millisecond access still required and data not yet infrequently accessed in a way that overcomes IA's per-GB retrieval fees, and Option B additionally uses single-AZ One Zone-IA (lower availability) plus Deep Archive that breaks the 5-hour retrieval limit, so neither is the best overall choice.

212.text:
Option B is correct because a CreationPolicy associated with a resource tells CloudFormation to wait for a specified number of success signals before marking the resource as CREATE_COMPLETE, with a configurable timeout. The instance signals CloudFormation (via cfn-signal) once software installation and configuration finish successfully; if the process stalls and no signal arrives within the timeout (set to 4 hours to cover the 2-3 hour install), CloudFormation fails the stack and rolls it back, exactly as required.

Option C is incorrect because DependsOn only controls the order in which resources are created and does not accept a timeout or wait for completion signals. Option A is incorrect because Conditions determine whether resources are created based on parameters and have no timeout or signaling behavior. Option D is incorrect because Metadata simply attaches arbitrary configuration data to a resource and does not influence stack success, signaling, or rollback on a timeout.

213.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Config managed rule eip-attached evaluates whether Elastic IP addresses are associated with a running instance or network interface, and it can run automatically as resource changes occur. Configuring automatic remediation with the AWS-ReleaseElasticIP Systems Manager Automation runbook, backed by an IAM role with the necessary permissions, means any unassociated EIP is detected and released automatically. This managed detect-and-remediate pattern requires no custom code, making it the most operationally efficient solution.

Option B is incorrect because EventBridge cannot use AWS Trusted Advisor as a target and the configuration described does not reliably trigger on EIP disassociation. Option C is incorrect because CloudFormation is for provisioning stacks, not for releasing an individual Elastic IP from an event payload, and the flow is convoluted. Option D is incorrect because the EC2 Instance State-change Notification event fires on instance state transitions, not on Elastic IP becoming unassociated, so the Lambda would not have a reliable trigger to release orphaned addresses.

214.text:
Option A is correct because the Access Denied error occurs since replicated objects in a cross-account setup are still owned by the source account, so the destination account owner cannot access them by default. Modifying the replication configuration to use the owner override (S3 Object Ownership / change object ownership to the destination bucket owner) transfers ownership of the replicas to the destination account, allowing that account to read the objects and resolving the error.

Option B is incorrect because scoping of the replication rule to a prefix affects which objects are replicated, not whether the destination owner can access already-replicated objects. Option C is incorrect because S3 Replication Time Control governs the time window in which replication completes; waiting longer does not change object ownership or permissions. Option D is incorrect because a storage class mismatch does not cause an Access Denied error on read; the root cause here is cross-account object ownership, which the owner override setting fixes.

215.text:
Option B is correct because the BurstBalance hitting 0 means the gp2 volume has exhausted its I/O credits and is throttled to its baseline IOPS, which is the source of the throughput problem. Changing the volume type to gp3 removes the burst-credit model entirely; gp3 delivers a baseline of 3,000 IOPS and 125 MiB/s included regardless of size, sustaining the required 3,000 IOPS for nightly builds. gp3 is also cheaper per GB than gp2, making it the most cost-effective fix.

Option A is incorrect because doubling the gp2 size raises baseline IOPS and credit accrual but costs more storage and still uses the burst model, so it is not the cheapest reliable fix. Option C is incorrect because Throughput Optimized HDD (st1) is optimized for large sequential throughput and cannot reliably sustain 3,000 IOPS for the random workload. Option D is incorrect because Provisioned IOPS SSD (io2) meets the IOPS but is significantly more expensive than gp3, so it is not the most cost-effective.

216.text:
Option A is correct because S3 Storage Lens provides the Incomplete Multipart Upload Bytes metric, giving the administrator visibility into the storage consumed by failed or abandoned multipart uploads across the bucket. Pairing this with an S3 Lifecycle policy that includes a rule to abort incomplete multipart uploads after 7 days automatically cleans up the leftover parts, reclaiming storage and reducing costs while satisfying both the metrics and the automatic-deletion requirements.

Option B is incorrect because S3 Intelligent-Tiering moves whole objects between access tiers and there is no "S3 Storage Lens policy" that deletes incomplete uploads; cleanup is done with a lifecycle rule. Option C is incorrect because the console Metrics tab does not surface incomplete multipart upload storage the way Storage Lens does, and writing a custom Lambda function to delete parts adds unnecessary operational overhead versus a native lifecycle rule. Option D is incorrect because storage class analysis examines object access patterns for tiering, not incomplete uploads, and a bucket policy cannot delete incomplete multipart uploads.

217.text:
Option B is correct because enabling sticky sessions (session affinity) on the Application Load Balancer target group binds a user's session to a single backend EC2 instance by issuing a load balancer-generated cookie. When session state is stored locally on each instance rather than in a shared store, requests routed to a different instance after each load balancing decision cause the session to be lost, forcing repeated logins. Sticky sessions ensure subsequent requests from the same client are sent to the same instance, preserving the session and stopping the repeated login prompts.

Option A is incorrect because adding a CloudFront distribution improves content caching and edge delivery but does not maintain session affinity to a specific backend instance, so logins would still be lost. Option C is incorrect because a spread placement group only influences the physical hardware placement of instances for availability and has no effect on session routing. Option D is incorrect because a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and does not natively manage HTTP application sessions; it also distributes connections without preserving application-level session state, so it would not resolve the login issue.

218.text:
Option B is correct because a service control policy (SCP) created in the AWS Organizations management account is the only mechanism that can centrally restrict the permissions of the root user across all member accounts. SCPs set the maximum available permissions for principals in member accounts, and root user actions are subject to SCP guardrails. By denying EC2 actions for the root user organization-wide, the administrators cannot perform those actions even with root credentials, meeting the requirement from a single, centralized control point.

Option A is incorrect because identity-based IAM policies do not apply to the account root user; the root user is not governed by IAM permission policies in the same way IAM principals are, so a deny would not constrain it. Option C is incorrect because AWS Config evaluates and reports on resource configuration compliance but cannot prevent or block API actions. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector is a vulnerability and network reachability assessment service; it does not detect root logins or enforce action-level restrictions on EC2.

219.text:
Option D is correct because for an instance to be managed by AWS Systems Manager it must have an IAM instance profile that grants the SSM Agent permission to communicate with the Systems Manager service. The standard Amazon Linux 2 AMI already includes SSM Agent preinstalled and running, so the missing piece is permissions. Attaching an instance profile containing the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore managed policy gives the agent the API permissions it needs to register, after which the instances appear in the Systems Manager console as managed instances.

Option A is incorrect because SSM Agent is already preinstalled and enabled on Amazon Linux 2, so reinstalling it does not address the actual cause, which is missing IAM permissions. Option B is incorrect because Systems Manager does not require manually imported ACM TLS certificates; the agent uses standard AWS API endpoints secured by the AWS SDK. Option C is incorrect because the ssm-user account is created automatically by Systems Manager Session Manager when needed and manually configuring sudoers does not grant the agent the API permissions required to register the instance.

220.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EventBridge natively receives EC2 instance state-change notifications as events, allowing a rule to match on instance state changes and route them to a target. Configuring the rule to publish to an Amazon SNS topic, with the administrators' email addresses subscribed to that topic, delivers email notifications automatically every time an instance changes state. This is the standard, fully managed event-driven pattern for state-change alerting.

Options A and B are incorrect because Amazon Route 53 is a DNS and routing service; routing policies direct DNS query traffic and have no capability to detect EC2 instance state changes or publish messages to SNS or SQS. Option D is incorrect because, although EventBridge can detect the state change, Amazon SQS is a message queue that does not send email notifications to subscribers; SQS requires a consumer to poll messages. Only SNS supports direct email subscriptions, so option C is the correct choice for delivering email to administrators.

221.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to store, encrypt, and automatically rotate database credentials. Defining an AWS::SecretsManager::Secret resource in the CloudFormation template creates and encrypts the credentials, and referencing them in the AWS::RDS::DBInstance resource with the resolve:secretsmanager dynamic reference securely injects the secret at deployment without exposing plaintext in the template. Secrets Manager natively supports automatic rotation for RDS, satisfying both the encryption and rotation requirements.

Option B is incorrect because the resolve:ssm-secure dynamic reference resolves SecureString parameters stored in SSM Parameter Store, not Secrets Manager secrets, so it cannot resolve an AWS::SecretsManager::Secret resource. Option C is incorrect because SSM Parameter Store standard parameters and the resolve:ssm reference do not provide built-in automatic credential rotation for RDS the way Secrets Manager does. Option D is incorrect because passing credentials as CloudFormation parameters referenced with Ref places the values in the template and stack inputs in plaintext, provides no encryption at rest in a managed secret store, and offers no automatic rotation.

222.text:
Option D is correct because server-side encryption with AWS KMS managed keys (SSE-KMS) encrypts the data at rest in Amazon S3 using a KMS key, so the company does not have to supply or manage its own encryption keys. Crucially, every use of the KMS key is logged in AWS CloudTrail, producing an auditable trail that shows when the key was used and which principal used it. This directly satisfies the requirement for encryption without customer-provided keys plus an access audit trail.

Option A is incorrect because client-side encryption with client-provided keys requires the company to manage and provide its own keys, which it explicitly does not want to do. Option B is incorrect because SSE-S3 uses S3-managed keys and does not generate per-request CloudTrail key-usage logs identifying who used a key, so it cannot provide the required audit trail. Option C is incorrect because SSE-C requires the customer to provide encryption keys with every request, contradicting the requirement to avoid providing keys, and it does not offer KMS-style key-usage auditing.

223.text:
Option A is correct because when CloudFront uses TLS to connect to a custom origin, an expired SSL/TLS certificate on the origin server causes the TLS handshake to fail, and CloudFront returns an HTTP 502 (Bad Gateway) error to viewers. Because the configuration worked for several months and then began failing, an expired certificate is the most likely cause. Examining the certificate's expiration date and replacing it if expired restores the secure connection between CloudFront and the origin and resolves the 502 errors.

Option B is incorrect because the origin certificate hostname must match the origin domain name CloudFront connects to, not the hostnames on the distribution; a mismatch there would have failed from the start rather than after months of working. Option C is incorrect because if inbound port 443 were closed, TLS to the origin would never have worked, contradicting the months of successful operation. Option D is incorrect because CloudFront is an AWS managed edge service and does not use customer-managed network ACLs for outbound connections to an on-premises origin, so adjusting NACLs would not apply.

224.text:
Option C is correct because an Auto Scaling lifecycle hook pauses an instance in a Terminating:Wait state before it is fully terminated, giving time to act on it. By adding a lifecycle hook and using Amazon EventBridge to match the lifecycle action and invoke the Lambda function, the administrator can capture diagnostics or trigger investigation logic before the unhealthy instance is removed. This prevents the loss of troubleshooting data that occurred when instances terminated too quickly.

Option A and option B are incorrect because instance scale-in protection only prevents Auto Scaling from terminating instances during scale-in events; it does not pause termination of instances that the group marks unhealthy, so the instance would still be replaced before the function could run. Additionally, options B and D are incorrect because Amazon Route 53 is a DNS service and cannot invoke a Lambda function in response to Auto Scaling lifecycle events. Only the EventBridge integration with a lifecycle hook reliably triggers the Lambda function at the right moment, making option C correct.

225.text:
Options B and C are correct because an InsufficientInstanceCapacity error means AWS does not currently have enough capacity for the requested instance type in the specified Availability Zone. Removing the explicit Availability Zone from the template (option B) lets AWS launch the instance in any AZ in the Region that has available capacity, increasing the chance of success.
