Changing to a different EC2 instance type (option C) targets a different capacity pool that may have availability, directly addressing the shortage.

Option A is incorrect because moving the EC2 definition into a separate CloudFormation template does not change the underlying capacity availability; the same capacity error would occur. Option D is incorrect because the AMI choice does not affect physical capacity; InsufficientInstanceCapacity relates to the instance type and AZ, not the image. Option E is incorrect because the validate-template command only checks template syntax and structure; it cannot detect or resolve runtime capacity shortages, which only surface during the actual launch attempt.

226.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes utilization metrics and provides EC2 rightsizing recommendations across instance families, helping the administrator match each workload to the instance type with the appropriate performance characteristics. Acting on these recommendations corrects the performance issues, and purchasing Compute Savings Plans then reduces cost with flexibility across instance families and sizes, which fits the strict budget policy because the commitment applies even as instance types change after rightsizing.

Option A is incorrect because purchasing regional Reserved Instances before rightsizing locks in commitments that may not match the optimal families, and although RIs can be exchanged, this is more operationally involved than Savings Plans flexibility. Option B is incorrect because zonal RIs are tied to specific Availability Zones and instance configurations, reducing flexibility while rightsizing across families. Option D is incorrect because On-Demand Capacity Reservations reserve capacity but do not by themselves lower cost; they bill at On-Demand rates unless combined with a Savings Plan or RI, making this less cost-efficient than the Compute Optimizer plus Savings Plans approach.

227.text:
Option D is correct because a service control policy (SCP) in AWS Organizations is the most operationally efficient way to enforce a Region restriction across all accounts from a single point. By creating an SCP that denies ec2:RunInstances in unauthorized Regions using a Region condition and attaching it at the organization root, every member account inherits the guardrail automatically, including future accounts, without requiring per-account configuration or maintenance.

Option A is incorrect because reactively terminating instances with CloudTrail, EventBridge, and Lambda allows resources to be created before remediation, leaving a compliance gap, and it requires building and maintaining infrastructure in every Region. Option B is incorrect because creating and attaching a managed IAM policy to all IAM groups in every account is operationally heavy, error-prone, and easy to bypass by principals not in those groups. Option C is incorrect because permissions boundaries must be configured per user in each account and only constrain the principals they are attached to, making them far less scalable and centrally enforceable than an organization-wide SCP.

228.text:
Option A is correct because increasing the size of an EBS volume in the EC2 console only expands the block device; the operating system and file system are not automatically aware of the additional capacity. To make the new space usable, the administrator must extend the file system using operating-system-level tools on the Windows instance, such as extending the partition and volume in Disk Management or with diskpart, so the file system recognizes and uses the expanded capacity.

Option B is incorrect because reattaching the EBS volume does not cause the file system to detect or use the additional space; the file system still needs to be extended. Option C is incorrect because rebooting the instance is unnecessary for modern Nitro-based and elastic volumes and, even after a reboot, the file system would still need to be extended to use the new capacity. Option D is incorrect because creating a new volume from a snapshot is an unnecessary and disruptive process that still would not make the additional space usable without extending the file system at the OS level.

229.text:
Option B is correct because the most secure cross-account access pattern uses IAM role assumption with temporary credentials rather than long-lived keys. Creating an IAM role in Account B that has permission to query the DynamoDB table, adding the EC2 instance's role as a trusted principal in that role's trust policy, and granting the EC2 instance's role sts:AssumeRole permission lets the application obtain short-lived credentials to access the table. This follows least privilege and avoids static secrets.

Option A is incorrect because an identity-based policy in Account A cannot grant access to resources in Account B without a corresponding cross-account trust, and the PassRole construct described does not enable cross-account DynamoDB access. Option C is incorrect because, while DynamoDB now supports resource-based policies, the described combination does not establish the secure trust relationship as cleanly as role assumption, and an identity policy alone in Account A cannot reach Account B's resource. Option D is incorrect because embedding static IAM access keys on the instance is the least secure approach; long-lived credentials can be leaked and are difficult to rotate, violating security best practices.

230.text:
Option B is correct because CloudFront honors origin cache-control directives, and the most direct way to vary the TTL per individual page is to have the origin return a Cache-Control: max-age header on each object. CloudFront uses that value to set the object's TTL, while still clamping it between the distribution's configured minimum and maximum TTL. This lets each page specify its own cache duration within the allowed range without any custom code.

Option A is incorrect because the CreateInvalidation API removes objects from the cache to force a refresh; it does not set or vary per-object TTLs and incurs operational and cost overhead. Option C is incorrect because adding a no-cache header through Lambda@Edge prevents caching rather than allowing each page to have a varying TTL within the configured bounds. Option D is incorrect because, although an Expires header can influence caching, applying it in a viewer response via a CloudFront function does not set the origin-driven object TTL that CloudFront caches against, so it does not achieve per-page TTLs constrained by the distribution's minimum and maximum settings the way the origin Cache-Control: max-age directive does.

231.text:
Options A and C are correct because both restore data that is no more than 24 hours old with minimal effort. Restoring the most recent automated snapshot (option A) to a new RDS DB cluster is a fully managed operation that produces a new cluster from a recent backup, and automated snapshots are taken daily within the retention window, satisfying the 24-hour freshness requirement. Creating a read replica and promoting it to a standalone cluster (option C) yields a new cluster containing current data, since the replica is continuously synchronized from the source before promotion.

Option B is incorrect because using native database backup tools to export to S3 and then importing into a new cluster is a manual, multi-step process with high operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because configuring AWS DMS to migrate data requires provisioning replication instances and tasks, adding significant setup and management effort. Option E is incorrect because using pg_dump and pg_restore through an intermediate EC2 instance is manual, time-consuming, and operationally heavy compared to the managed snapshot restore and read replica promotion options.

232.text:
Option C is correct because for an EC2 instance to be a managed instance in AWS Systems Manager, it must have an attached IAM instance profile that grants the SSM Agent permission to communicate with the service. Even when the agent is installed and running and users have proper permissions, instances missing an instance profile with Systems Manager permissions will not register as managed. Ensuring every instance has an instance profile that includes Systems Manager access fixes the issue so all instances appear and can be inventoried.

Option A is incorrect because tags are used for grouping and targeting instances, not for granting the underlying permission required for an instance to be managed; missing tags would not exclude instances from being managed. Option B is incorrect because IAM Access Analyzer identifies resources shared externally and does not manage or remediate Systems Manager agent registration. Option D is incorrect because an interface VPC endpoint is only required when instances lack internet access to reach Systems Manager endpoints; since some instances in the same subnet are managed, network connectivity is not the blocker, pointing to missing instance profiles instead.

233.text:
Option A is correct because S3 Batch Operations is purpose-built to perform a single operation across large numbers of S3 objects with minimal effort. By specifying the replace-all-object-tags operation, the administrator can update the tags on hundreds of objects in one managed job, supplying a manifest of objects and letting S3 handle the execution, retries, and completion reporting. This is the most operationally efficient approach for bulk tag replacement.

Option B is incorrect because scripting the AWS CLI to fetch each object's tags, save them, delete them through Batch Operations, and then retag them is unnecessarily complex and involves multiple manual steps. Option C is incorrect because iterating with the AWS CLI to get, remove, and retag each object individually is slow, error-prone, and does not scale efficiently to hundreds of objects. Option D is incorrect because copying all objects to another bucket, retagging the copies, and deleting the originals is a heavy, risky data-movement approach that can change object metadata and incurs extra cost and complexity compared to the native batch tagging operation.

234.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Config is designed to record configuration changes to resources such as security groups, maintaining a documented configuration history. By delivering configuration snapshots and history files to an Amazon S3 bucket and creating an Amazon SNS topic for configuration change notifications, the administrator gets both the durable audit trail and email alerts when a subscribed email address receives notifications from the SNS topic. This directly meets the requirement for a documented trail plus change notifications.

Options A and D are incorrect because Amazon Detective is a security investigation service that analyzes and visualizes findings; it does not record resource configuration changes or store configuration history. Option D additionally pairs Detective with SNS but still relies on the wrong recording service. Option B is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager Change Manager is a change request and approval workflow tool, not a service that automatically records all security group configuration changes. Option A is also incorrect because subscribing an email address to an Amazon SQS queue does not deliver email; SQS requires a consumer to poll messages, whereas SNS supports direct email subscriptions.

235.text:
Option B is correct because the Aurora Backtrack feature lets you rewind an Aurora MySQL DB cluster to a prior point in time in place, without restoring from a snapshot or creating a new cluster. Enabling Backtrack with a target window that covers the reset interval allows a scheduled EventBridge rule to invoke a Lambda function daily that performs a backtrack operation, instantly returning the database to its populated baseline state. This is the most operationally efficient solution because it avoids provisioning new clusters or moving data.

Option A is incorrect because restoring a snapshot to a new cluster and deleting the previous cluster each day involves significant time, endpoint changes, and operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because exporting a snapshot to S3 and restoring it daily is a slow, heavy data-movement process. Option D is incorrect because point-in-time restore creates a brand-new cluster each day and requires deleting the old one and updating connection endpoints, which is far more disruptive and operationally costly than backtracking in place.

236.text:
Option A is correct because the capacity-optimized allocation strategy launches Spot Instances from the pools with the most available capacity, which reduces the likelihood of interruptions and improves the chance that capacity is available at scheduled scaling times. Combining this strategy with more instance types in the Auto Scaling group gives the group access to a greater number of Spot capacity pools, further increasing the probability that instances launch on time and run with fewer interruptions.

Option B is incorrect because, while capacity-optimized is the right strategy, simply increasing instance size narrows the available capacity pools rather than broadening them, which does not improve launch reliability and can make capacity harder to find. Option C is incorrect because the lowest-price allocation strategy prioritizes cost over capacity, drawing from pools that are more likely to be reclaimed, which increases interruptions. Option D is incorrect for both reasons: it uses the interruption-prone lowest-price strategy and increases instance size, reducing pool diversity, so it fails to address the goals of timely launches and fewer interruptions.

237.text:
Option C is correct because the AWS Config managed rule restricted-ssh continuously evaluates security groups for inbound SSH rules open to all IP addresses and flags noncompliant groups. Attaching automatic remediation that runs the AWS Systems Manager Automation AWS-DisablePublicAccessForSecurityGroup runbook removes the offending public access automatically, and an Amazon EventBridge rule notifies the SysOps team when the rule reports noncompliance. This combination meets both the notification and automatic remediation requirements using managed services with minimal custom code.

Option A is incorrect because the custom Lambda function described removes all inbound rules on all ports, which is overly destructive and would break legitimate access rather than remediating only the noncompliant SSH rule. Option B is incorrect because using a CloudTrail metric filter and alarm to trigger a Lambda function that simply removes the rule is more complex and less reliable than the purpose-built Config rule, and removing the entire rule may be too broad. Option D is incorrect because it relies on CloudTrail metric filters and alarms with manual runbook actions, which is operationally heavier and less precise than the managed Config rule with built-in remediation.

238.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Machine Image IDs are Region-specific; an AMI ID that exists in us-east-1 does not exist in us-west-2. When a CloudFormation template hardcodes an AMI ID from one Region and is deployed in another Region, the EC2 resource fails to launch because the referenced AMI cannot be found there. This is a classic cross-Region portability issue, typically solved by using mappings or SSM parameters to resolve the correct AMI per Region.

Option A is incorrect because resource tags are arbitrary key-value labels that are not Region-specific and do not cause an instance launch to fail. Option C is incorrect because the cfn-init script runs after the instance is provisioned to perform configuration; a cfn-init failure would not prevent the EC2 instance from being created in the first place. Option D is incorrect because IAM is a global service and IAM users are not created per Region, so IAM user Region scoping is not a real cause of the launch failure.

239.text:
Option B is correct because creating a CloudFront origin access identity (OAI) and granting it permission to read objects in the S3 bucket lets you restrict the bucket policy so that only the OAI can access the objects. By removing the open public permissions and allowing only the OAI, users can no longer reach objects through direct S3 URLs and must go through the CloudFront distribution, ensuring access occurs only via CloudFront URLs while resolving the Trusted Advisor open-access warning.

Option A is incorrect because SSE-S3 only encrypts object data at rest and does nothing to control who can access objects or to force access through CloudFront. Option C is incorrect because CloudFront distributions do not assume IAM users, and assigning an IAM user does not restrict S3 access exclusively to CloudFront requests. Option D is incorrect because CloudFront does not use a generic IAM role to access an S3 origin in this scenario; the supported mechanism for restricting an S3 origin to CloudFront is the origin access identity (or origin access control), not an IAM role granted in the bucket policy.

240.text:
Option B is correct because CloudTrail log file integrity validation creates a digitally signed digest file for each log delivery period, containing hash values of the delivered log files. The administrator can use these digest files, which are signed with a private key and verifiable with a public key, to confirm that log files have not been modified, deleted, or tampered with after delivery to the S3 bucket. This is the AWS-native, purpose-built mechanism for proving log file integrity.

Option A is incorrect because streaming logs to CloudWatch Logs provides a secondary copy and search capability but does not cryptographically prove that the files in S3 are unaltered. Option C is incorrect because cross-Region replication and S3-managed encryption protect against data loss and provide encryption at rest, but they do not detect or prove tampering of the log contents. Option D is incorrect because S3 server access logging records requests made to the bucket for auditing access patterns, but it does not validate the integrity or detect modification of the CloudTrail log files themselves.

241.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon GuardDuty is a managed threat detection service that continuously analyzes CloudTrail event logs, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to identify suspicious activity. Its IAM finding types, such as UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/ConsoleLoginSuccess, specifically flag console logins from unusual or anomalous geographic locations, directly meeting the requirement to detect potential unauthorized logins from multiple locations with no infrastructure to manage.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Cognito provides user identity and authentication for application end users and does not monitor IAM console login behavior across an AWS account. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs vulnerability and network reachability assessments on workloads such as EC2 instances and container images; it does not analyze login activity or geographic access patterns. Option C is incorrect because AWS Config evaluates resource configuration compliance against rules, and the iam-policy-blacklisted-check rule inspects attached policies, not real-time console login events from different locations.

242.text:
Option A is correct because the ActiveConnectionCount metric is published by the Elastic Load Balancer to Amazon CloudWatch and directly represents the number of concurrent connections the load balancer is handling, which closely tracks the number of users connected to the application. Creating a target tracking or step scaling policy against this ELB-generated metric lets the Auto Scaling group scale precisely with user demand.

Option B is incorrect because mem_used is a custom memory metric collected by the CloudWatch agent on the instances, not a metric generated by the ELB, and memory utilization does not directly reflect the number of connected users. Option C is incorrect because scheduled scaling adjusts capacity at predefined times and cannot respond dynamically to fluctuating user connections. Option D is incorrect because you cannot deploy custom scripts onto a managed ELB; the load balancer is an AWS-managed service, and the connection count is already exposed natively through the ActiveConnectionCount metric without custom instrumentation.

243.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Config can detect noncompliant resources across all accounts in an organization and supports automatic remediation through AWS Systems Manager Automation documents. A managed rule can flag security groups that allow 0.0.0.0/0 inbound, and the associated remediation action can replace the offending source with the approved intranet CIDR block, providing the detection plus automatic correction the company requires.

Option B is incorrect because attaching an IAM deny policy to every user is operationally fragile, only prevents future creation, does not remediate existing groups, and does not cover roles or automation principals. Option C is incorrect because building a custom Lambda function to continuously inspect and rewrite security groups duplicates capabilities AWS Config already provides and adds significant operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because while an SCP can block creating noncompliant security groups, SCPs are guardrails that deny actions; they cannot perform automatic remediation to change an existing 0.0.0.0/0 source to the approved CIDR block.

244.text:
Option A is correct because instances in two different Regions reside in separate VPCs, so inter-Region VPC peering establishes private connectivity between them over the AWS backbone without internet exposure. Security groups filter inbound traffic by source, so adding the eu-central-1 instances' private IP address range to the inbound rule of the database security group permits the connection, since security group references cannot span Regions in a peering connection.

Option B is incorrect because security group rules control allowed traffic on inbound rules for the receiving resource, and referencing a peer security group is not supported across Regions; adding it to an outbound rule of the database also does not authorize incoming connections. Option C is incorrect because a Site-to-Site VPN is intended for hybrid on-premises connectivity, adds tunnel management overhead, and outbound database rules do not authorize inbound connections. Option D is incorrect because, although the inbound direction is right, cross-Region peering does not allow referencing a security group from another Region, so the IP range must be used instead.

245.text:
Option A is correct because the CloudWatch agent supports modular configuration files that can be merged at runtime. By creating a second configuration file that targets only the DHCP logs and using AWS Systems Manager Run Command to restart the agent with the append-config option on the 50 affected instances, the new configuration is added on top of the existing baseline without disrupting it. Run Command applies the change centrally at scale, avoiding any manual per-instance work.

Option B is incorrect because logging in to each instance to write and maintain PowerShell scripts is manual, error prone, and not operationally efficient across a fleet. Option C is incorrect because running the configuration wizard interactively on every instance is a manual process that does not scale to 50 hosts. Option D is incorrect because selecting an advanced detail level in the wizard captures standard operating system metrics and logs, not the specific application DHCP log files, and it still requires manual execution on each instance.

246.text:
Option D is correct because the AWS Personal Health Dashboard (now part of AWS Health) provides account-specific alerts and proactive notifications about events that affect your resources, including scheduled hardware maintenance and instances that will require a reboot or retirement. It surfaces the exact list of affected instances with no agents to deploy or custom monitoring to build, giving the least administrative overhead.

Option A is incorrect because deploying a third-party monitoring solution adds licensing, configuration, and maintenance overhead and still would not receive AWS internal hardware maintenance schedules. Option B is incorrect because failed system status checks indicate that an issue is already occurring, not that maintenance is upcoming, so it is reactive rather than predictive. Option C is incorrect because monitoring CloudTrail for StopInstances API calls only records when an instance is stopped, and AWS-initiated maintenance events are surfaced through AWS Health, not as user-driven API calls in CloudTrail.

247.text:
Option B is correct because patch baselines define auto-approval rules, including the auto-approval delay measured in days after a patch's release date. To apply different delays of 2 days for development and 5 days for production, you need two patch baselines, each with its own delay, and two patch groups created with tags so the correct baseline is associated with each group. A single maintenance window can then service all instances within the required 2-hour window.

Option A is incorrect because the auto-approval delay is configured in the patch baseline, not in the patch group, so a single baseline cannot enforce two different delays. Option C is incorrect for the same reason and because the delay is a baseline property rather than a maintenance window property; two windows are also unnecessary. Option D is incorrect because it tags only development instances and relies on predefined baselines for production, which would not enforce the specific 5-day production delay the requirement demands.

248.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Trusted Advisor publishes service limit (quota) check results as CloudWatch metrics, so you can create a CloudWatch alarm for each of the five metrics with a threshold at 60% of the quota. When an alarm enters the ALARM state, it triggers an Amazon SNS topic, and SNS delivers the email notification to subscribed addresses, fully satisfying the per-quota email alerting requirement.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon SQS is a message queuing service and cannot deliver email notifications; it has no email subscription protocol. Option C is incorrect because the AWS Service Health Dashboard reports the status of AWS services broadly and does not monitor your individual Trusted Advisor service quota metrics, and SQS still cannot send email. Option D is incorrect because, although SNS can send email, the Service Health Dashboard does not track per-account service quota usage thresholds, so it cannot trigger notifications when usage exceeds 60%.

249.text:
Option A is correct because an AWS CloudFormation stack set lets you deploy the same template across multiple accounts and Regions in an organization from a single operation. The template creates the standard CloudWatch alarms in each account, and each alarm references a centralized Amazon SNS topic in the logging account whose access policy grants publish permissions to all member accounts, providing the centralized alerting solution the company needs.

Option B is incorrect because deploying a stack individually in each account is manual and not centralized; it also creates a separate SNS topic per account rather than a single consolidated alerting destination. Option C is incorrect because a custom Lambda function on a cron in every account adds significant operational overhead and reinvents native CloudWatch alarm functionality. Option D is incorrect because a change set is only a preview of proposed modifications to a single stack and is not a mechanism for organization-wide deployment, and routing alerts to a single SES identity does not provide centralized cross-account metric alerting.

250.text:
Option D is correct because AWS OpsWorks (Stacks and the configuration management offerings) is built around Chef and lets you organize resources into stacks and layers while running your existing Chef recipes for configuration and deployment. This allows the company to migrate and continue using its current Chef recipes with minimal rework, making it the most operationally efficient choice.

Option A is incorrect because manually building and operating a self-managed Chef server on an EC2 instance via CloudFormation adds substantial administrative overhead for patching, scaling, and availability. Option B is incorrect because CloudFormation does not natively understand or run Chef recipes; it provisions AWS infrastructure with its own templating model and has no concept of Chef layers. Option C is incorrect because Elastic Beanstalk with the Docker platform deploys containerized applications and is not designed to consume or execute Chef recipes for configuration management.

251.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Budgets lets you create separate cost budgets, each filtered by the specific AWS services a team owns (storage, compute, or database). You set the finance-defined budget amount and a forecasted (projected) cost threshold, and configure email recipients per budget so each team is alerted when its projected spend will exceed the limit. AWS Budgets does not provision compute, storage, or database resources, so it adds no extra costs.

Option A is incorrect because building Cost and Usage Report processing with Lambda and SNS incurs additional compute and storage costs and significant operational effort. Option B is incorrect for the same cost reason and because SQS cannot send email notifications. Option C is incorrect because a single combined budget covering all services cannot send team-specific alerts based on each team's owned services; separate filtered budgets are required to alert each team independently.

252.text:
Option D is correct because Tape Gateway supports automatic tape creation, where you configure a minimum number of available tapes, a barcode prefix, and a tape pool (such as Glacier or Deep Archive). The gateway then automatically creates new virtual tapes whenever the available count drops below the configured minimum, ensuring there is always enough tape for backup jobs and preventing the recurrence of Not Enough Space errors with no custom code.

Option A is incorrect because building and maintaining a custom Lambda function on an hourly schedule reinvents the native auto-create feature and adds operational overhead. Option B is incorrect because it requires installing the CloudWatch agent, log filters, alarms, and a Lambda function, which is far more complex and is reactive rather than preventive. Option C is incorrect because adding a second Tape Gateway with manual SNS notifications does not automatically create tapes and still relies on a person to intervene before space runs out.

253.text:
Option A is correct because an Auto Scaling group warm pool maintains a pool of pre-initialized instances that have already completed the lengthy boot process and machine-specific cache creation, then keeps them in the Stopped state to minimize cost. When a scale-out event occurs, instances transition from the warm pool to the InService state almost immediately, allowing the application to absorb sudden, unpredictable traffic surges much faster than launching fresh instances.

Option B is incorrect because manually starting 100 instances and storing data on instance store volumes is not durable, since instance store data is lost when an instance stops, and it is not an automated, repeatable solution. Option C is incorrect because increasing the instance warmup time only delays counting new instances toward metrics; it does not reduce the actual boot time. Option D is incorrect because predictive scaling relies on historical, recurring traffic patterns, but the advertisement timing is unknown and irregular, so predictions would not reliably anticipate the surges.

254.text:
Option D is correct because a Route 53 weighted routing policy distributes traffic across multiple records in proportion to assigned weights. By configuring the original resource record with a weight of 80 and the new resource record with a weight of 20, Route 53 sends approximately 80% of requests to the existing site and 20% to the new version, exactly matching the limited rollout requirement.

Option A is incorrect because a failover routing policy only sends traffic to the secondary record when the primary fails its health check; it cannot split traffic by percentage. Option B is incorrect because multivalue answer routing returns multiple healthy records and relies on client-side selection, which does not reliably guarantee a precise 20% split. Option C is incorrect because latency-based routing directs users to the Region with the lowest latency and does not use weights; mixing weight values into a latency policy does not produce a percentage-based traffic split.

255.text:
Options A and C are correct because when a VPC has exhausted its private IPv4 addresses, you can associate a secondary IPv4 CIDR block with the VPC to expand its address space, and then create a new subnet that draws from that additional CIDR block. Launching the instances into the new subnet provides the available private IPv4 addresses needed, since instances always receive addresses from their subnet's range.

Option B is incorrect because associating an IPv6 CIDR block does not provide private IPv4 addresses, and the requirement is specifically about exhausted IPv4 space. Option D is incorrect because you cannot modify or resize the primary CIDR block of an existing VPC; you can only add secondary CIDR blocks. Option E is incorrect because the CIDR block of an existing subnet cannot be modified or expanded after the subnet is created, so it cannot relieve the address shortage.

256.text:
Option C is correct because S3 Transfer Acceleration uses the globally distributed Amazon CloudFront edge locations to route uploads over the optimized AWS backbone network, significantly improving transfer speeds for clients that are geographically distant from the bucket's Region. Enabling it on the new bucket and having developers use the bucketname.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com endpoint directly addresses the lower upload performance experienced from certain geographic areas.

Option A is incorrect because ElastiCache for Redis is an in-memory cache for application data and has no role in accelerating S3 object uploads. Option B is incorrect because CloudFront is optimized for content delivery (downloads/reads) to viewers and is not the appropriate mechanism for accelerating uploads into S3, which Transfer Acceleration is purpose-built for. Option D is incorrect because while multipart upload improves throughput for large files by parallelizing parts, using Region-specific endpoints does not overcome the latency caused by long geographic distance the way Transfer Acceleration's edge network does.

257.text:
Option B is correct because deploying a duplicate CloudFormation stack in us-west-2 gives you a second Region with its own ELB, and Route 53 alias records can target an ELB directly. By creating A records configured with a failover routing policy and Route 53 health checks, you designate the us-east-1 ELB as the primary and the us-west-2 ELB as the secondary, so traffic automatically fails over to the other Region if the primary becomes unhealthy, achieving multi-Region high availability.

Option A is incorrect because an SOA record defines the authoritative DNS zone parameters and cannot contain multiple ELB IP addresses or perform failover. Option C is incorrect because attaching EC2 instances in another Region to an existing ELB is not possible, since a load balancer operates only within its own Region and VPC. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering provides network connectivity but does not perform DNS-based failover, and Route 53 does not route on VPCs as primary/secondary records.

258.text:
Option B is correct because CloudWatch dashboards are defined by a JSON document, and CloudFormation supports an AWS::CloudWatch::Dashboard resource whose DashboardBody property accepts that JSON. By exporting the existing dashboard's JSON from the console and embedding it in the template's DashboardBody, every deployment of the stack automatically creates its own dashboard, fully automating the process across Regions.

Option A is incorrect because scripting the AWS CLI put-dashboard command and running it manually after each stack creation is a separate, manual step rather than integrated automation within the deployment. Option C is incorrect because there is no intrinsic Ref pattern that points an AWS::CloudWatch::Dashboard resource at an existing dashboard's ID; the resource creates a new dashboard from its body definition. Option D is incorrect because specifying only a DashboardName without a DashboardBody does not define the dashboard's widgets and metrics, so it would not reproduce the intended dashboard content for each deployment.

259.text:
Option B is correct because a Route 53 alias record can point directly to an AWS resource such as a Network Load Balancer using the resource's DNS name, automatically tracking the NLB's IP addresses. Alias record queries to AWS resources are not charged by Route 53, making it the most cost-effective way to map the company's domain name to the NLB while still resolving at the zone apex if needed.

Option A is incorrect because an AAAA record maps a name to an IPv6 address, and the NLB uses no Elastic IPs while the requirement is simply to route the domain to the load balancer, not to manually manage addresses. Option C is incorrect because a CAA record only specifies which certificate authorities may issue certificates for the domain and does not route traffic. Option D is incorrect because a CNAME record cannot be used at a zone apex, and CNAME queries are billed, making it less cost-effective than an alias record that resolves to the NLB at no query charge.

260.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Backup natively integrates with Amazon EFS and stores recovery points in a backup vault from which you can perform partial restore jobs. A partial restore lets you specify individual files or directories to recover rapidly without restoring the entire file system, which directly meets the requirement to recover specific corrupted files quickly and cost-effectively.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon DLM manages EBS snapshots and AMIs, not EFS file system backups, and S3 Glacier retrievals are slow, conflicting with the need for rapid file recovery. Option B is incorrect because maintaining a second EFS file system in another Region plus continuous DataSync replication is more expensive and operationally heavy than AWS Backup for this use case. Option C is incorrect because AWS Backup does not store EFS backups in a customer S3 Glacier vault, and Glacier retrieval is not the rapid, granular file-level recovery the company requires.

261.text:
Option A is correct because you cannot convert an existing standard queue into a FIFO queue in place, so the company must create a new SQS FIFO queue. FIFO queues require every message to carry a message group ID to define ordering, so the application must be updated to supply one. Since the message bodies are unique, enabling content-based deduplication lets SQS generate the deduplication ID automatically from the body hash, satisfying the FIFO deduplication requirement without manual IDs.

Option B is incorrect because the DelaySeconds parameter only delays message delivery and does not address the mandatory message group ID or deduplication that FIFO queues require. Option C is incorrect because SQS does not allow modifying a standard queue's type to FIFO, and turning off content-based deduplication would force the application to supply a deduplication ID for unique messages unnecessarily. Option D is incorrect because the queue type cannot be modified in place, and sending identical bodies with DelaySeconds neither enables proper deduplication nor provides the required message grouping.

262.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CloudFormation StackSets is purpose-built to deploy a single template across multiple accounts and Regions from one central administration account. Using service-managed permissions integrates StackSets with AWS Organizations so the necessary cross-account roles are created and managed automatically, allowing the SysOps administrator to administer and run deployments centrally across the required accounts and Regions.

Option A is incorrect because storing the template in S3 with Cross-Region Replication only copies the template file; it does not orchestrate provisioning of stacks into multiple accounts or Regions. Option B is incorrect because nested stacks loaded from S3 buckets in target Regions still deploy within a single account and Region context and do not provide multi-account, multi-Region orchestration. Option C is incorrect because nested stacks are a modularization technique within one stack operation and cannot deploy independently into separate accounts and Regions the way StackSets can.

263.text:
Option C is correct because Aurora Auto Scaling automatically adds or removes Aurora Replicas based on a target metric such as the average number of database connections. Setting a target tracking policy on the DatabaseConnections metric at 195 causes Aurora to add replicas before connections exceed 200 and remove them when demand falls, automatically scaling read capacity in response to changing user demand and preventing the read performance degradation.

Option A is incorrect because Aurora multi-master is designed for scaling write availability across multiple writers, not for automatically scaling read connection capacity, and it adds complexity. Option B is incorrect because you cannot dynamically toggle a provisioned cluster into Aurora Serverless mode based on connection counts; serverless is a distinct cluster configuration, not an on-demand switch. Option D is incorrect because increasing the replica instance size is a manual vertical scaling action that does not scale automatically with demand and offers no elasticity when connections fluctuate.

264.text:
Option D is correct because the AWS best practice for granting EC2-hosted applications access to other services is to create an IAM role with the required DynamoDB permissions and attach it to the instance through an instance profile. The application then obtains temporary, automatically rotated credentials from the instance metadata service, eliminating the need to store long-term secrets and providing secure, least-privilege access to the DynamoDB table.

Option A is incorrect because embedding long-term access keys is a security anti-pattern, and access keys are associated with IAM users, not assigned to an instance profile. Option B is incorrect because an EC2 key pair is used for SSH or RDP authentication to the instance and conveys no AWS API permissions. Option C is incorrect because an IAM user represents a long-term identity with static credentials and cannot be attached to an instance profile; only IAM roles can be assumed by EC2 instances through an instance profile.

265.text:
Option A is correct because an interface VPC endpoint for Amazon S3, powered by AWS PrivateLink, creates an elastic network interface with a private IP address inside the VPC. Because the Direct Connect connection terminates into the VPC, traffic from the on-premises web portal can reach S3 entirely over the AWS private network and the Direct Connect link, never traversing the public internet. This directly satisfies the compliance requirement, whereas a gateway endpoint would not work for on-premises traffic because gateway endpoints are only reachable from within the VPC via route tables.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Network Firewall filters and inspects traffic at the VPC boundary; it does not provide a private path to S3 or an internal S3 address. Option C is incorrect because the path-style endpoint only changes the URL format of the request and still resolves to public S3 endpoints over the internet. Option D is incorrect because network ACLs are stateless allow/deny rules at the subnet level; they cannot redirect traffic to a private S3 address, and no such routable internal address exists for them to use.

266.text:
Option C is correct because Service Quotas integrates natively with Amazon CloudWatch and can emit utilization metrics for supported quotas, including EC2 instance quotas. From the Service Quotas console you can create a CloudWatch alarm tied to the quota utilization metric, set the threshold at 70%, and have the alarm publish to an Amazon SNS topic when it enters the ALARM state. This is fully managed, requires no custom code, and is the most operationally efficient way to be notified as the company approaches its instance service quota.

Option A is incorrect because, while functional, writing and maintaining a Lambda function plus an EventBridge schedule introduces unnecessary custom code and operational overhead compared with the built-in Service Quotas CloudWatch integration. Option B is incorrect for the same overhead reason and because the CloudWatch Metrics API does not provide the applied quota value needed for the comparison. Option D is incorrect because the CPUUtilization metric measures instance load, not how many instances are running against the service quota, so it cannot detect approaching the quota limit.

267.text:
Option D is correct because a custom resource (declared with a type name beginning with Custom:: , such as Custom::MyCustomType) lets CloudFormation invoke a Lambda function or SNS topic during stack create, update, and delete operations. This allows a single logical resource to provision and orchestrate multiple AWS services or perform actions that no native resource type supports, with full lifecycle handling through the CloudFormation console. The custom resource provider sends a response signal so CloudFormation knows when the work succeeded or failed.

Option A is incorrect because AWS::EC2::Instance with cfn-init only configures software on a single EC2 instance; it does not create a single resource spanning multiple services. Option B is incorrect because AWS::OpsWorks::Instance provisions one OpsWorks-managed instance and is unrelated to combining multiple services into one resource. Option C is incorrect because AWS::SSM::Document defines an automation or command document; it does not create or delete a composite multi-service resource through the CloudFormation lifecycle the way a custom resource does.

268.text:
Option B is correct because Application Load Balancer health checks succeed only when the target responds on the protocol and port the target group expects, returning the configured success status code on the configured health check path. A common cause of failing instances is that the application listens on a different port or protocol than the listener and target group are configured to probe, so the SysOps administrator should first verify the application is actually serving on the expected protocol and port. Misaligned ports, missing security group rules, or wrong health check paths are the typical culprits.

Option A is incorrect because Auto Scaling groups operate within a single Region and its Availability Zones; they are not configured to span all AWS Regions, so this is not a valid health check cause. Option C is incorrect because listener priority controls rule evaluation order for routing, not whether instances pass health checks. Option D is incorrect because the maximum instance count affects scaling capacity, not why already-launched instances fail their health checks.

269.text:
Option A is correct because each VPC subnet uses a route table that must contain an explicit route directing on-premises (corporate) CIDR traffic to the virtual private gateway. When the administrator created subnets in the new Availability Zone, those subnets were associated with a route table that lacks the route to the virtual private gateway, so the new resources have no path back to the data center. Adding a route for the on-premises networks pointing to the virtual private gateway (or enabling route propagation) restores connectivity, since the existing VPN and gateway already handle traffic correctly for the original subnets.

Option B is incorrect because Site-to-Site VPN connections are not scoped to Availability Zones; a virtual private gateway serves the entire VPC, so no support request is needed. Option C is incorrect for the same reason and adds an unnecessary, duplicate VPN connection. Option D is incorrect because replacing the working VPN with Direct Connect is a costly, time-consuming change that does not address the actual problem, which is the missing route table entry.

270.text:
Options A and C are correct because together they enforce encrypted, properly identified HTTPS delivery. Setting the Viewer Protocol Policy to redirect HTTP to HTTPS for each cache behavior ensures any plaintext request is automatically upgraded to TLS, so all viewer traffic is encrypted in transit. Adding www.example.com as an alternate domain name (CNAME) on the distribution and selecting the custom ACM SSL certificate lets CloudFront serve valid HTTPS for that domain instead of only the default cloudfront.net certificate, which is required for the certificate to match the requested hostname.

Option B is incorrect because allowing both HTTP and HTTPS still permits unencrypted requests, violating the requirement that all traffic be encrypted in transit. Option D is incorrect because an AWS WAF web ACL filters malicious requests and provides no encryption capability. Option E is incorrect because Origin Shield adds an extra caching layer to reduce origin load and improve cache hit ratios; it has nothing to do with enforcing TLS for viewer connections.

271.text:
Option C is correct because once a Lambda function is attached to private subnets in a VPC, it loses default internet access and must route outbound traffic through a NAT gateway. The solution places NAT gateways in the public subnets, adds routes in the private subnet route tables pointing internet-bound traffic (0.0.0.0/0) to the NAT gateways, and attaches the function to the private subnets so it can reach the RDS instance directly within the VPC. A security group with appropriate outbound rules then allows the function to reach both the database and the internet through the NAT gateways.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda functions in a VPC do not receive Elastic IP addresses, and placing the function in public subnets does not grant internet access without a NAT gateway. Option B is incorrect for the same reasons; Lambda ENIs are not assigned public IPs, so a public subnet alone provides no outbound internet path. Option D is incorrect because resources in private subnets cannot route through an internet gateway, and a security group cannot be associated with a subnet.

272.text:
Option A is correct because enabling S3 server access logging provides a detailed audit record of all access attempts to the bucket, satisfying the logging requirement. Configuring an S3 event notification for the bucket that targets an Amazon SNS topic and selecting the s3:ObjectRemoved (DeleteObject) event type delivers an immediate alert to the risk team whenever an object is deleted. This is a native, event-driven approach that fires in near real time without polling or custom infrastructure.

Option B is incorrect because running an EC2 instance with a daily cron job to scan downloaded logs introduces operational overhead and delays notification by up to a day, failing the immediate-notification requirement. Option C is incorrect because S3 data events are not natively written to CloudWatch Logs without CloudTrail data event logging, and a CloudWatch alarm on its own does not detect specific object delete events. Option D is incorrect because it relies on a self-managed EC2 instance and a daily comparison job, which is operationally heavy and far too slow to provide immediate alerts.

273.text:
Option B is correct because the Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler automatically increases or decreases the number of pod replicas for a workload based on observed metrics such as a target CPU utilization percentage. By scaling out additional pods when traffic and CPU rise, the application maintains capacity to handle the expected surge in requests without crashing, and it scales back in when load subsides. This is a native Kubernetes feature on Amazon EKS, requiring minimal configuration and the least administrative overhead.

Option A is incorrect because building and load balancing a second EKS cluster is operationally complex and expensive, far exceeding what a temporary traffic increase requires. Option C is incorrect because migrating the application off EKS to EC2 and back again is a large, error-prone effort with significant overhead. Option D is incorrect because the Vertical Pod Autoscaler resizes the CPU and memory requests of existing pods rather than adding replicas; it typically restarts pods to apply changes and is less suited to absorbing a high volume of concurrent requests than horizontal scaling.

274.text:
Option D is correct because S3 Versioning protects objects from accidental overwrite and deletion by retaining previous versions and inserting delete markers rather than removing data permanently. Pairing versioning with an S3 Lifecycle policy that expires noncurrent versions after 90 days permanently deletes the old versions on the required schedule. Because S3 Lifecycle and versioning operate entirely within the same bucket and Region, the requirement that objects remain in the original Region is automatically met.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager manages EBS snapshots and AMIs, not S3 objects, so it cannot govern noncurrent S3 versions. Option B is incorrect because AWS Backup does not provide the in-place versioning protection against overwrite that the requirement implies, and it adds unnecessary complexity for simple lifecycle expiration. Option C is incorrect because Cross-Region Replication copies objects to a different Region, directly violating the requirement that objects reside within the same Region as the original bucket.

275.text:
Option A is correct because turning on S3 Block Public Access at the account level applies a centralized, definitive override that prevents any bucket or object in the account from being made public, regardless of individual bucket policies or ACLs. This is the simplest and most reliable control to enforce the security policy that prohibits public exposure of S3 data, and it blocks both existing and future public configurations across all buckets.

Option B is incorrect because an EventBridge rule can detect or react to changes but does not, by itself, prevent objects from being made public; it would require additional remediation logic and still allows a window of exposure. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses EC2, container, and Lambda vulnerabilities and network reachability; it does not scan or reset S3 bucket ACLs. Option D is incorrect because S3 Object Lambda transforms data returned by GET requests through a Lambda function; it does not inspect or modify bucket-level public access settings.

276.text:
Options B and D are correct because both prevent CloudFront from offloading traffic from the ALB. If the public DNS record still points to the ALB instead of the CloudFront distribution domain name, viewers connect directly to the ALB and bypass CloudFront entirely, so the origin sees no reduction in load. Separately, if the default, minimum, and maximum TTLs are all set to 0 seconds, CloudFront caches nothing and forwards every request to the origin, meaning the ALB continues to serve all requests despite the distribution being in place.

Option A is incorrect because origin access identity is used to restrict access to an Amazon S3 origin, not an ALB, so it is irrelevant to this configuration. Option C is incorrect because a blocked security group would cause CloudFront requests to fail rather than result in the ALB still serving traffic normally. Option E is incorrect because sticky sessions on the target groups only affect how requests are pinned to backend instances and have no bearing on whether CloudFront caches or serves content.

277.text:
Option C is correct because AWS CloudFormation StackSets is purpose-built to deploy and manage a common CloudFormation template across multiple accounts and Regions from a single management or delegated administrator account. With AWS Organizations integration, the management account can automatically provision resources to existing and newly added member accounts, making it the correct service for automating resource provisioning organization-wide.

Option A is incorrect because a change set only previews the modifications a stack update would make within a single account; it does not deploy across multiple accounts. Option B is incorrect because nested stacks organize related resources within one parent stack in a single account and do not provide cross-account deployment. Option D is incorrect because AWS SAM is a framework for defining and deploying serverless applications to a single account and Region; it lacks the native multi-account orchestration that StackSets provides.

278.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Machine Images are Region-scoped resources, so an AMI created in eu-west-2 is not directly usable in other Regions. The CopyImage operation copies the AMI (and its associated snapshots) to us-east-1 and us-east-2, creating new AMIs with their own IDs in those Regions. The administrator can then launch EC2 instances from the copied AMIs in each target Region.

Option B is incorrect because making the AMI public exposes it to all AWS accounts and still does not make it available in other Regions, since AMIs remain Region-bound. Option C is incorrect because sharing an AMI grants other accounts permission to use it within the same Region; sharing does not move an AMI across Regions, and the scenario involves the same account in additional Regions. Option D is incorrect because AMIs are not used by copying them into an arbitrary S3 bucket; that is not how AMI distribution across Regions works, and assigning S3 permissions does not create launchable AMIs in the new Regions.

279.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Security Hub natively runs the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark as a managed security standard, and through its integration with AWS Organizations you can designate a delegated Security Hub administrator account, configure new organization accounts to automatically be enrolled as member accounts, and enable the CIS standard centrally. This requires no custom scripting and continuously scans all accounts, making it the most operationally efficient solution.

Option A is incorrect because relying on a script that sends and accepts invitations for every new account is the legacy manual approach and adds ongoing operational overhead compared with Organizations auto-enrollment. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses software vulnerabilities and network exposure; it does not run the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark. Option C is incorrect because GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes logs for malicious activity and does not perform CIS benchmark compliance scans, and it likewise relies on manual invitation scripting in this option.

280.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Config provides the managed s3-bucket-logging-enabled rule, which continuously evaluates whether server access logging is turned on for new and existing S3 buckets. By attaching an automatic remediation action (using an SSM Automation document) to this rule, AWS Config detects noncompliant buckets and automatically enables logging without manual intervention. This managed, continuous, self-remediating approach delivers the requirement with the least operational effort.

Option A is incorrect because building detection on CloudTrail plus a custom Lambda remediation function requires writing and maintaining code and event handling, which is more operationally intensive than a managed Config rule. Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor offers best-practice recommendations but does not automatically remediate or enable S3 access logging. Option D is incorrect because CloudWatch metrics do not report S3 bucket logging configuration state, and pairing them with a custom Lambda function again adds unnecessary development and maintenance overhead.

281.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon CloudWatch does not collect memory utilization or disk space by default; these are custom metrics that require the CloudWatch agent installed and configured on each EC2 instance. To allow the agent to publish metrics and logs to CloudWatch, the instances need permissions granted through an IAM role attached to the instance (via an instance profile), which provides temporary, automatically rotated credentials following AWS best practices.

Option A is incorrect because AWS does not automatically install or configure the CloudWatch agent on instances; the agent must be deployed manually or through automation. Option C is incorrect because attaching an IAM user to an instance is not a valid mechanism; EC2 instances obtain permissions through IAM roles, and embedding long-term user credentials is a security anti-pattern. Option D is incorrect because security groups control network traffic to and from the instance and do not grant the API permissions needed to write metrics or logs to CloudWatch.

282.text:
Options A and B are correct because both directly relieve read pressure on the single RDS instance. Adding a read replica creates an asynchronously updated copy of the database that can serve read-only queries, offloading read traffic from the primary instance during peak periods and improving overall read scalability. Modifying the application to use Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached lets frequently requested query results be served from an in-memory cache, dramatically reducing repeated read load on the database and improving response times.

Option C is incorrect because migrating to DynamoDB is a major re-architecture requiring a non-relational data model and application rewrite, not a performance tuning action. Option D is incorrect because moving the database to self-managed EC2 increases operational overhead and enhanced networking improves packet throughput, not database read scalability. Option E is incorrect because a Multi-AZ deployment provides high availability and failover with a synchronous standby; that standby does not serve read traffic, so it does not address the read-overutilization problem.

283.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon RDS Proxy maintains a pool of established database connections and sits between the application and the RDS instance. During a Multi-AZ failover, RDS Proxy detects the failover and routes connections to the new primary, reducing failover times by bypassing DNS propagation delays that applications normally wait on. AWS documents that RDS Proxy can cut failover times significantly, easily meeting the requirement to decrease failover time by at least 10%, while also improving application resiliency to database connection storms.

Option A is incorrect because increasing the instance size improves compute and memory capacity but does not shorten the failover process. Option B is incorrect because running in a single Availability Zone removes Multi-AZ failover entirely, eliminating high availability rather than improving failover time. Option C is incorrect because a cross-Region read replica requires manual promotion and DNS changes, which lengthens recovery time and is intended for disaster recovery, not for reducing Multi-AZ failover duration.

284.text:
Option B is correct because user-defined cost allocation tags do not automatically appear in Cost Explorer; they must first be activated in the Billing console under Cost Allocation Tags. Until the company activates the specific user-defined tags, AWS does not apply them to cost and usage data, so they cannot be used to filter or group views in Cost Explorer. After activation, the tags begin appearing for billing data going forward.

Option A is incorrect because there is no 30-day waiting period for tag-based filtering; tags become available shortly after they are activated for cost allocation, and 20 days would have been sufficient if activation had occurred. Option C is incorrect because creating an AWS Cost and Usage Report is not a prerequisite for using tags in Cost Explorer; the two features are independent. Option D is incorrect because creating a usage budget in AWS Budgets is unrelated to whether tags can be used to filter Cost Explorer views.

285.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 Inventory produces a scheduled flat-file report (CSV, ORC, or Parquet) listing all objects in a bucket along with selected metadata fields, including encryption status. By creating an S3 Inventory configuration and including the encryption status field, the security team can verify which current objects are encrypted across the entire bucket without writing or running any custom code, making it the most operationally efficient solution at scale.

Option A is incorrect because writing and maintaining a custom script that iterates over every object adds development effort and runs slowly against large buckets. Option C is incorrect because granting an IAM user read access does not produce any encryption-status verification; the team would still have to inspect objects manually. Option D is incorrect because using the AWS CLI to list objects returns object keys but not a consolidated encryption-status report, and it would still require additional scripting to evaluate each object, making it less efficient than S3 Inventory.

286.text:
Option B is correct because storing the content in an S3 bucket with public access blocked and using an origin access identity ensures the bucket is reachable only through CloudFront and never directly from the internet. Restricting access with CloudFront signed URLs then limits delivery to authorized users, since only requests carrying a valid signed URL (generated for specific users with an expiration) can retrieve the content. This combination enforces both private origin access and per-user authorization.

Option A is incorrect because leaving public access unblocked on the bucket allows users to bypass CloudFront and access objects directly, defeating the access restriction. Option C is incorrect because field-level encryption protects sensitive form data submitted by viewers; it does not restrict which users can download content. Option D is incorrect because, although signed cookies can authorize users, leaving the bucket public without an OAI lets users bypass CloudFront entirely, so the unauthorized-access requirement is not met.

287.text:
Option A is correct because AWS CloudFormation StackSets lets you deploy stack instances of a single template across multiple accounts and multiple Regions from one operation. By integrating StackSets with AWS Organizations, the SysOps administrator can turn on AWS Config in every account and every Region efficiently and consistently, including automatic deployment to accounts added to the organization later, satisfying the requirement with minimal operational effort.

Option B is incorrect because StackSets deploy stack instances from templates, not stack policies; a stack policy merely protects resources from updates and cannot turn on AWS Config. Option C is incorrect because service control policies define permission guardrails on what actions accounts may take; they cannot provision or enable a service like AWS Config. Option D is incorrect because a hand-written AWS CLI script run from the management account is error-prone, requires custom maintenance, and does not automatically cover newly added accounts, making it far less operationally efficient than StackSets.

288.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Budgets is purpose-built to track costs, including data transfer costs filtered by usage type, and to send alerts when actual or forecasted spend reaches a configured percentage of a budgeted threshold. Creating a cost budget for data transfer and setting an alert at 75% of the budgeted amount delivers email notifications to the distribution list automatically, directly meeting the requirement with native, low-overhead functionality.

Option A is incorrect because building a Cost and Usage Report and analyzing it in Athena, then wiring up an alarm, is far more complex than necessary and does not natively trigger on a percentage of a cost threshold. Option B is incorrect because a CloudWatch billing alarm monitors total estimated account charges and cannot granularly track inter-Region data transfer cost against a defined budget percentage. Option D is incorrect because VPC flow logs capture network traffic metadata, not dollar costs, so a Lambda function parsing them cannot reliably alert on transfer cost thresholds.

289.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon FSx File Gateway provides low-latency, on-premises access to fully managed Windows file shares hosted in Amazon FSx for Windows File Server. It caches frequently accessed data locally so on-premises users get LAN-like performance, while the authoritative data lives in AWS and is natively accessible to in-cloud resources. Because FSx for Windows File Server is built on Windows Server and supports SMB and Active Directory, it is the purpose-built service for Windows file shares.

Option A is incorrect because an S3 File Gateway presents objects through an NFS/SMB interface backed by S3, which is not a true managed Windows file system and is not optimized for Windows file-share semantics. Option B is incorrect because Direct Connect only provides a dedicated network link; it does not provide a managed file system or local caching. Option C is incorrect because AWS DataSync performs one-way or scheduled bulk data transfers between storage systems; it does not present live, low-latency shared file access on premises or keep the shares continuously synchronized for interactive use.

290.text:
Option A is correct because an S3 Lifecycle rule can automatically abort and delete incomplete multipart uploads after a defined number of days using the AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload action. This cleans up the orphaned parts that accumulate when uploads never complete, reclaiming the 5 GB of storage and reducing the object count automatically without ongoing manual effort. It is the AWS-recommended, hands-off way to manage incomplete multipart upload artifacts.

Option B is incorrect because forcing clients to use a particular TransferUtility may improve future upload reliability but does nothing to remove the existing incomplete uploads already present in the bucket. Option C is incorrect because enabling S3 Versioning preserves multiple versions of objects and would increase, not decrease, stored data; it does not address incomplete multipart uploads. Option D is incorrect because an S3 Object Lambda Access Point transforms data returned on GET requests; it has no capability to detect or delete incomplete multipart uploads.

291.text:
Option C is correct because CloudWatch metrics explorer lets you build dynamic, tag-based visualizations that automatically include any resource matching a filter. By filtering on the aws:autoscaling:groupName tag and selecting the CPUUtilization metric, the visualization continuously reflects every instance in the Auto Scaling group, including newly launched instances, with no custom code to maintain. Adding this visualization to a dashboard satisfies the requirement in the most operationally efficient way.

Option A is incorrect because building a Lambda function triggered by Auto Scaling notifications to rewrite the dashboard introduces significant custom code and operational overhead to maintain. Option B is incorrect because running custom scripts on each instance to stream metrics is brittle, requires per-instance configuration, and duplicates the CPUUtilization metric EC2 already publishes. Option D is incorrect because filtering by instance state does not scope the view to the Auto Scaling group and would include unrelated instances, failing to track group membership accurately.

292.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes utilization data and provides specific right-sizing recommendations for both Amazon EC2 instances and AWS Lambda functions, including suggested instance types and Lambda memory settings. Acting on these recommendations directly reduces cost by eliminating over-provisioned resources, which precisely matches the goal of cutting EC2 and Lambda spend with minimal effort.

Option A is incorrect because querying the Cost and Usage Report with Athena helps you analyze and attribute spend but does not by itself produce actionable right-sizing recommendations for EC2 and Lambda. Option B is incorrect because an AWS Budgets alert only notifies you when spending crosses a threshold; it provides no mechanism to actually reduce cost. Option C is incorrect because purchasing Reserved Instances may lower EC2 costs but does nothing for Lambda and risks locking in capacity for workloads that may not be right-sized, whereas Compute Optimizer addresses both services with data-driven guidance.

293.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Control Tower Account Factory standardizes and automates account provisioning. Using an Account Factory template (or product), new accounts are created with a predefined baseline, networking, and governance guardrails enforced through AWS Organizations and service control policies. This delivers scalable, repeatable, self-service account creation that saves time and resources, exactly as required.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Elastic Beanstalk deploys and manages application environments; it does not provision AWS accounts or apply organizational governance. Option B is incorrect because OpsWorks (configuration management) combined with CloudFormation can build infrastructure but does not natively automate multi-account creation with governance guardrails the way Control Tower does, and OpsWorks is largely deprecated. Option C is incorrect because AWS Config evaluates resource compliance and Service Catalog distributes approved products; neither service provisions new AWS accounts, so this combination cannot meet the account-creation requirement.

294.text:
Option B is correct because a CloudWatch Logs metric filter extracts numeric values from log events and publishes them as a CloudWatch custom metric. Once the latency field is converted into a metric, you can graph and alarm on percentile statistics such as p90 over time, which is exactly what is needed to monitor the latency distribution rather than just averages.

Option A is incorrect because CloudWatch Contributor Insights identifies top contributors (such as the highest-latency hosts) from log data but does not produce a continuous p90 time-series statistic of a numeric field. Option C is incorrect because a subscription filter streams matching log events to a destination like Lambda or Kinesis Data Firehose for further processing; it does not compute percentile statistics. Option D is incorrect because CloudWatch Application Insights provides automated monitoring and problem detection for application stacks but does not extract a custom latency field and chart its p90 statistic.

295.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CloudFormation StackSets integrated with AWS Organizations lets you deploy and manage the same IAM role definitions across many accounts from a single template, with automatic deployment to existing and newly added accounts via service-managed permissions. This eliminates the manual, per-account role creation and provides centralized, repeatable lifecycle management, making it the most operationally efficient solution.

Option A is incorrect because plain CloudFormation templates would still have to be deployed and updated in each account individually, which is exactly the manual overhead the team wants to remove. Option B is incorrect because AWS Directory Service provides managed Active Directory and does not automate the creation or management of IAM roles across accounts. Option C is incorrect because AWS Resource Access Manager shares specific resources (such as subnets or Transit Gateways) across accounts; it does not create or manage IAM roles.

296.text:
Option C is correct because the error message states that the account quota allows for 0 more running instances, which is a Service Quotas (formerly service limit) restriction on running On-Demand instances for that instance family. Requesting a quota increase through Service Quotas in the AWS Management Console raises the limit so the Auto Scaling group can launch the instances it needs to reach its minimum size.

Option A is incorrect because there is no per-service EC2 spending limit in Billing and Cost Management that would block instance launches; spending controls do not produce this quota error. Option B is incorrect because EC2 instance running quotas are managed through Service Quotas, not an editable EC2 console settings page. Option D is incorrect because the Rebalance action redistributes existing instances across Availability Zones and does nothing to raise the running-instance quota that is causing the launch failure.

297.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics canaries run scripted, browser-based checks that follow the same routes and actions as a real customer against the public URL on a recurring schedule. Canaries emit a SuccessPercent metric, so creating a CloudWatch alarm that triggers when SuccessPercent falls below 95% and publishes to an SNS topic delivers the required notification with native, low-maintenance tooling.

Option B is incorrect because Route 53 health checks only test endpoint reachability; they do not follow multi-step customer routes or actions, and there is no HealthCheckPercentageHealthy metric tied to scripted user journeys. Options C and D are incorrect because building custom Lambda functions to probe endpoints reinvents Synthetics with far more code and operational overhead; option C cannot easily compute a 95% success rate across runs, and option D's per-path functions and custom metrics add unnecessary complexity compared to a single canary.

298.text:
Option C is correct because creating an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the S3 bucket as the origin caches the static content at edge locations close to users. Subsequent requests are served from the cache, which both reduces latency for global users and dramatically lowers the read request load on the S3 bucket, directly addressing the high read rate observed.

Option A is incorrect because moving the bucket to a single closer Region only helps users near that Region and still concentrates all read load on one bucket. Option B is incorrect because cross-Region replication copies data for durability and locality but does not provide caching, and it adds storage cost while still requiring clients to read directly from S3.
