Option D is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache is an in-memory cache for application data accessed by your compute tier; it is not a content delivery mechanism for serving static web objects to end users directly from S3.

299.text:
Option B is correct because the jobs are interruptible, can run at any time, and restart from the beginning if they fail, which is the ideal profile for Spot Instances. A one-time Spot request runs the job on spare capacity at up to a 90% discount versus On-Demand, and if the instance is reclaimed the job simply restarts, meeting the requirement most cost-effectively.

Option A is incorrect because Reserved Instances require a long-term commitment and are best for steady, always-on workloads, not flexible batch jobs that run intermittently. Option C is incorrect because defined-duration Spot Instances (Spot blocks) have been deprecated and were also more expensive than standard Spot, so they are not the most cost-effective choice. Option D is incorrect because mixing On-Demand with Spot adds On-Demand cost unnecessarily; since the workload tolerates interruption and restart, pure Spot capacity is cheaper while still meeting the fault-tolerance requirement.

300.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon RDS supports cross-Region automated backups, which can be enabled by simply modifying the DB instance to replicate automated backups (snapshots and transaction logs) to another Region. RDS handles encryption and the ongoing copy process natively, so this is the most operationally efficient way to keep regular backups available in a second Region with no custom scripting.

Option B is incorrect because creating a read replica in another Region and snapshotting it adds the cost and management of a running replica purely for backups, which is more overhead than necessary. Option C is incorrect because AWS DMS is designed for data migration and continuous replication, not for maintaining routine database backups, and it introduces significant configuration complexity. Option D is incorrect because you cannot disable encryption on an already-encrypted RDS instance, and doing so would violate security best practices; cross-Region snapshot copies of encrypted databases are supported without removing encryption.

301.text:
Option C is correct because a latency-based routing policy in Amazon Route 53 automatically directs each user to the AWS Region that provides the lowest network latency. By changing the record to latency routing and creating two latency records, one aliasing the us-west-2 ALB and one aliasing the ap-southeast-2 ALB, users are sent to the closest-performing endpoint while still resolving the same app.anycompany.com URL.

Option A is incorrect because simply adding a second value to a simple-routing alias record is not supported in a way that routes by latency; simple routing returns values without latency awareness. Option B is incorrect because geolocation routing routes based on the user's geographic location, not measured latency, and requires mapping locations explicitly, which is less accurate for minimizing latency. Option D is incorrect because multivalue answer routing returns multiple records for client-side load distribution and health checking but does not select the lowest-latency endpoint.

302.text:
Options C and D are correct because HTTP Layer 7 status codes are recorded by the components that terminate and serve HTTP requests. ALB access logs capture detailed request entries including the ALB and target HTTP status codes for each connection, and CloudFront access logs record the HTTP status code returned to viewers for every request served by the distribution. Together these two sources expose the Layer 7 status codes needed for the investigation.

Option A is incorrect because VPC Flow Logs record IP traffic metadata such as source/destination, ports, and accept/reject decisions at the network layer, with no HTTP status codes. Option B is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail logs AWS API management and data events, not application HTTP responses. Option E is incorrect because Amazon RDS logs contain database engine output such as query, error, and audit logs, which do not include web-layer HTTP status codes.

303.text:
Option C is correct because an origin access identity (OAI) is a special CloudFront principal that you assign to the distribution and reference in the S3 bucket policy. By restricting the bucket policy to allow access only to the OAI and removing public access, S3 will serve objects only for requests that come through the CloudFront distribution, ensuring users cannot reach the bucket directly.

Option A is incorrect because allowing GetObject from the distribution alone does not by itself bind requests to CloudFront's identity the way an OAI (or OAC) does, and the wording does not establish the trusted CloudFront principal needed to lock down direct access. Option B is incorrect because Origin Shield is a caching layer that reduces origin load; it does not restrict who can access the bucket. Option D is incorrect because an OAI is assigned to the CloudFront distribution, not to the S3 bucket, and securing access is done through the bucket policy rather than a custom origin header.

304.text:
Options A and B are correct because an InsufficientInstanceCapacity error means AWS does not currently have enough of the requested instance type available in the targeted Availability Zone. Changing to a different (and more widely available) instance type increases the chance that capacity exists, and configuring the Auto Scaling group across multiple Availability Zones lets it launch instances in any AZ that has capacity, both directly remediating the shortage.

Option C is incorrect because EBS volume size has no bearing on EC2 compute capacity availability. Option D is incorrect because increasing the maximum group size only raises the ceiling on instance count; it does not help when AWS lacks capacity for the current request. Option E is incorrect because a service quota increase addresses account limits that produce a different error (your quota allows 0 more instances), not the capacity shortage indicated by InsufficientInstanceCapacity.

305.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides secure shell access to EC2 instances without bastion hosts, open inbound ports, or SSH keys. The latest AWS-provided Windows and Amazon Linux AMIs include the SSM Agent, and instances in a private subnet can be reached through Session Manager. Adding an allow statement for the ssm:StartSession action to the existing IAM role grants the engineers exactly the permissions they need to connect.

Option B is incorrect because associating Elastic IPs and opening security group ports exposes private instances to the internet and is a poor security practice. Option C is incorrect because a bastion host adds an instance to manage, and ec2:CreateVpnConnection does not grant interactive instance access. Option D is incorrect because exposing SSH and RDP through an internet-facing Network Load Balancer is insecure, and ec2:CreateRoute manages route tables rather than providing instance login access.

306.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudTrail can be configured with a trail that logs data events for Amazon S3, capturing object-level API activity such as GetObject, PutObject, and DeleteObject across all S3 objects. This provides the detailed, auditable record of S3 API activity the company requires for its critical data.

Option A is incorrect because S3 bucket request metrics in CloudWatch provide aggregate operational statistics, not a log of individual API calls. Option C is incorrect because S3 server access logging records best-effort web request logs delivered to a bucket, but the requirement to record all S3 API activity is met more precisely and reliably by CloudTrail data events, which is the AWS-recommended approach for API auditing. Option D is incorrect because IAM Access Analyzer for S3 evaluates bucket access policies to surface buckets shared externally; it does not record object access logs or API activity.

307.text:
Option A is correct because instance scale-in protection prevents Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling from terminating a specific instance during a scale-in event. By calling the Auto Scaling API to enable scale-in protection on the instance at the start of message processing and disabling it once processing completes, the instance is protected throughout the up-to-12-hour job while still allowing normal scaling of other instances, exactly meeting the requirement.

Option B is incorrect because the OldestInstance termination policy only changes which instance is chosen for termination; it does not prevent an actively processing instance from being terminated. Option C is incorrect because OldestLaunchConfiguration likewise only influences selection order during scale-in, offering no protection for in-progress work. Option D is incorrect because suspending the Launch and Terminate processes affects the entire Auto Scaling group rather than a specific instance, disabling normal scaling and replacement behavior, and is not the targeted, per-instance protection this scenario needs.

308.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Config required-tags managed rule continuously evaluates resources and marks EC2 instances that lack the department tag as noncompliant. Configuring automatic remediation with the AWS-TerminateEC2Instance automation document terminates those noncompliant instances, and because Config reacts to configuration changes, remediation happens in near-real time, satisfying the policy enforcement requirement.

Option B is incorrect because an EventBridge rule that merely sends events to an SNS topic notifies operators but does not automatically terminate noncompliant instances. Option C is incorrect because granting tagging permissions and changing shutdown behavior to terminate does not detect missing tags or enforce termination of untagged instances. Option D is incorrect because Systems Manager and the AWS-StopEC2Instances document only stop instances rather than terminate them, and it does not address tag-based compliance detection, so it fails to meet the requirement to terminate untagged resources.

309.text:
Option D is correct because S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads to S3 by routing data over Amazon CloudFront's globally distributed edge locations and AWS's optimized backbone network to the bucket's Region. Using the acceleration endpoint when uploading gigabytes of files each day increases throughput and upload speed, especially over long distances, which is exactly the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because a CloudFront distribution with the GET method accelerates content delivery (downloads) to viewers, not uploads into S3. Option B is incorrect because ElastiCache is an in-memory data cache for applications and cannot be enabled to cache or accelerate S3 uploads. Option C is incorrect because AWS Global Accelerator improves availability and performance for applications fronted by endpoints such as ALBs, NLBs, or EC2; it is not the integrated mechanism for accelerating uploads to an S3 bucket, which is precisely what S3 Transfer Acceleration provides.

310.text:
Option A is correct because adding a Retain deletion policy to the DynamoDB table resource tells AWS CloudFormation to keep that resource when the stack is deleted. When the stack is removed, the Lambda function and API Gateway are deleted as expected, but the DynamoDB table and its data are preserved, exactly meeting the requirement.

Option B is incorrect because a Snapshot deletion policy is not supported for DynamoDB in CloudFormation and would not retain the live table itself; snapshot policies apply to resources like RDS and EBS. Option C is incorrect because stack termination protection prevents the entire stack from being deleted, which contradicts the goal of deleting the stack while keeping only the table. Option D is incorrect because a Deny on dynamodb:DeleteTable in the application's IAM policy governs application runtime actions, not how CloudFormation handles resource removal during stack deletion.

311.text:
Option D is correct because to host a static website on Amazon S3 with a custom domain through Route 53, the S3 bucket name must exactly match the domain name in the record set (for example, www.example.com). S3 routes website requests to the bucket whose name matches the Host header, so a mismatch between the bucket name and the DNS record name prevents the website from being served.

Option A is incorrect because CloudFront is optional for S3 static website hosting and is not required for Route 53 to route the domain to the bucket. Option B is incorrect because Route 53 record sets do not use IAM roles to access S3; access for static website hosting is controlled by the bucket policy and public access settings. Option C is incorrect because Route 53 is a global service and does not need to be in the same Region as the S3 bucket, so a Region mismatch is not the cause.

312.text:
Options B and D are correct because securing HTTPS traffic to a website behind an Application Load Balancer is done by terminating TLS at the ALB. You create a public certificate in AWS Certificate Manager, since the website is internet-facing and uses a public domain name, and then attach that ACM certificate to an HTTPS listener on the ALB. ACM-issued public certificates are free and auto-renewing, making this the standard, low-overhead approach.

Option A is incorrect because you do not attach the certificate to each EC2 instance when using an ALB; TLS is terminated at the load balancer, which avoids per-instance certificate management. Option C is incorrect because a private ACM certificate is issued by AWS Private CA and is not trusted by public browsers, so it is unsuitable for a public retail website. Option E is incorrect because ACM public certificates cannot be exported for manual installation, and manual deployment is unnecessary when the ALB integrates directly with ACM.

313.text:
Option D is correct because the CloudWatch agent's configuration file supports an append_dimensions field that lets you add custom dimensions to the metrics the agent collects. You define these dimensions directly in the agent configuration JSON, and the agent automatically attaches them to every metric it publishes, which is the native, supported mechanism for enriching agent-collected metrics with additional dimensions.

Option A is incorrect because writing a custom shell script to extract dimensions duplicates functionality the agent already provides and adds unnecessary operational overhead and maintenance. Option B is incorrect because an EventBridge rule routes and reacts to events; it does not modify or add dimensions to metrics the CloudWatch agent collects, and SNS is a notification service, not a metric processor. Option C is incorrect because CloudTrail records API activity rather than the EC2 instance metrics in question, and a Lambda function that copies data into a CloudWatch Logs group neither uses the CloudWatch agent nor adds custom dimensions to the application's metrics.

314.text:
Options D and E are correct because hosting a static website on S3 requires both content and public read access. You create an index.html document, enable static website hosting on the bucket, and upload the index document so the website endpoint can serve it (Option E). To make objects publicly readable, you turn off Block all public access and attach a bucket policy that allows the s3:GetObject action to the public principal (Option D), which is the recommended way to grant anonymous read access.

Option A is incorrect because the redirect requests functionality is used to redirect to another bucket or host, not to serve a webpage from the current bucket. Option B is incorrect because there is no WEBSITE permission in an S3 ACL; ACL permissions are READ, WRITE, READ_ACP, and WRITE_ACP. Option C is incorrect because the AuthenticatedUsers grantee grants access to any signed-in AWS user, not anonymous public website visitors, so it would not allow general internet users to view the page.

315.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Secrets Manager provides built-in, fully managed automatic rotation for Amazon RDS credentials. You can configure a rotation interval such as 30 days, and Secrets Manager natively integrates with RDS using AWS-provided rotation Lambda functions that it manages for you, so you do not have to author or maintain rotation code. This delivers the least operational overhead while meeting the RDS integration requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Systems Manager Parameter Store secure strings do not provide native automatic credential rotation; you would have to build rotation yourself. Option C is incorrect because storing credentials in a file in S3 is insecure and requires you to write and maintain a custom Lambda rotation function, adding significant overhead. Option D is incorrect because, although Secrets Manager is the right service, manually deploying and maintaining your own Lambda rotation function is unnecessary when Secrets Manager already provides managed RDS rotation, so it adds avoidable operational overhead.

316.text:
Option B is correct because setting the function's reserved concurrency to 0 immediately throttles all invocations of the Lambda function. With zero concurrency, Lambda stops executing new invocations, which breaks the recursive S3-to-Lambda loop right away without deleting data or introducing errors, and it is fully reversible once the developer fixes the code.

Option A is incorrect because deleting all objects destroys data and does not stop new puts from re-triggering the function, so the loop can resume. Option C is incorrect because changing the bucket policy to deny the function access would cause the function to fail with access-denied errors rather than stopping cleanly, and it can have broader side effects on legitimate access. Option D is incorrect because publishing a new version does not stop invocations; the existing trigger continues to invoke the function and the recursive loop persists.

317.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes CloudWatch utilization metrics, including EBS volume IOPS and throughput, and produces right-sizing recommendations automatically. After you opt in and allow time for metrics to accumulate, Compute Optimizer surfaces findings that identify over-provisioned EBS volumes and recommends cost-optimized configurations, which is the most operationally efficient approach because it requires no custom analysis or per-instance tooling.

Option A is incorrect because manually reviewing monitoring graphs for consumed versus provisioned space measures capacity, not IOPS and throughput performance, and is labor-intensive and error-prone at scale. Option B is incorrect because changing instance types to EBS-optimized addresses instance-to-volume bandwidth rather than analyzing volume performance or generating cost recommendations. Option D is incorrect because installing the fio benchmarking tool on every instance and crafting workload configuration files is highly manual, intrusive to production, and does not scale across a large fleet, contradicting the operational-efficiency requirement.

318.text:
Option D is correct because a CloudWatch alarm that is constantly in the ALARM state typically indicates that the alarm is not receiving the expected metric data. When data is missing and the alarm is configured to treat missing data as breaching, or the metric source is misconfigured so that no valid datapoints arrive, the alarm cannot transition to OK and remains stuck in ALARM. Verifying that the metric is actually being published resolves the issue.

Option A is incorrect because, although alarms do continuously evaluate metrics against thresholds, that normal behavior would not keep an alarm permanently in ALARM unless the data itself were the problem. Option B is incorrect because alarms automatically transition back to OK when the metric returns within threshold; they do not require manual disabling. Option C is incorrect because CloudWatch alarms reset themselves based on incoming metric data and do not depend on application logic to clear the ALARM state.

319.text:
Option D is correct because an Application Load Balancer operates at Layer 7 and supports content-based routing, including path-based routing rules that inspect the URL path of each request. The ALB can register the legacy EC2 instances in one target group and the Lambda function in a Lambda target group, then use listener rules to forward requests to either target based on the URL path, which directly meets the transition routing requirement.

Option A is incorrect because a Gateway Load Balancer is designed for inserting third-party virtual appliances such as firewalls and operates at Layer 3, with no URL path awareness. Options B and C are incorrect because a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and routes based on protocol and port, not HTTP URL paths; it cannot match path strings or regular expressions and cannot target a Lambda function for HTTP path routing.

320.text:
Option D is correct because the ALB's HTTPS listener uses a security policy that defines the set of SSL/TLS protocols and ciphers it negotiates with clients. By updating the listener to a security policy that includes the older ciphers and protocol versions that legacy browsers require, the SysOps administrator restores compatibility in a single configuration change, which is the most operationally efficient solution and does not require replacing certificates or restructuring the environment.

Option A is incorrect because the issue is cipher and protocol negotiation, not the certificate itself, so a new ACM certificate would not help legacy browsers connect. Option B is incorrect because deploying a second ALB with a different domain and a custom certificate adds significant complexity and cost without addressing the underlying negotiation mismatch. Option C is incorrect because removing the ALB and installing certificates on each instance abandons centralized TLS termination, increases management overhead, and is far less operationally efficient than adjusting the security policy.

321.text:
Option B is correct because a simple scaling policy with default settings honors a cooldown period after a scaling activity. When the RequestCountPerTarget metric breaches the threshold, the Auto Scaling group launches one instance and then waits for the default cooldown period to elapse before responding to subsequent alarm breaches, even though the metric exceeded the limit twice within 180 seconds. This prevents the group from over-scaling on closely spaced breaches.

Option A is incorrect because simple scaling does not launch an instance on every breach; the cooldown blocks additional scaling actions until it expires. Option C is incorrect because Auto Scaling does not send an alert to the ALB to rebalance traffic in lieu of scaling; load balancing and scaling are independent mechanisms. Option D is incorrect because the Auto Scaling group does not pause to redistribute existing traffic before scaling; it adds capacity according to its policy and cooldown rather than deferring on traffic distribution.

322.text:
Option A is correct because to let on-premises servers resolve names in a Route 53 private hosted zone, you create a Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint in the VPC. The inbound endpoint provides IP addresses in the VPC that on-premises DNS servers can send queries to, and after ensuring security groups and routing permit traffic over the Direct Connect connection, you configure the on-premises DNS server to conditionally forward queries for the private zone's domain to those inbound endpoint IPs.

Option B is incorrect because an outbound endpoint forwards queries from the VPC to external resolvers; it is the wrong direction for resolving AWS-hosted private zones from on-premises. Options C and D are incorrect because editing the private hosted zone with TXT or PTR records does not establish DNS forwarding from on-premises, and the VPC base+2 resolver address is not directly reachable from on-premises networks for these queries, so neither approach enables on-premises resolution of the private hosted zone.

323.text:
Option C is correct because a timeout when connecting to an EC2 instance commonly indicates that the security group does not permit the inbound SSH traffic. Security groups are stateful, so allowing inbound TCP port 22 from the administrator's IP address is sufficient for the response traffic to return automatically. Adding this inbound rule lets the SSH connection reach the instance and resolves the timeout.

Option A is incorrect because route tables direct subnet traffic to gateways and do not contain entries for individual source IP addresses to enable SSH access. Option B is incorrect because, while network ACLs are stateless, SSH requires an inbound allow rule for port 22; an outbound rule alone does not permit the initial inbound connection. Option D is incorrect because security groups are stateful, so an outbound SSH rule is unnecessary for inbound connections, and adding it does not address the missing inbound port 22 permission that causes the timeout.

324.text:
Options C and D are correct because deleting an object protected by S3 Object Lock in governance mode requires both the proper IAM permission and an explicit bypass instruction. The principal must assume a role that has the s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission (Option C), and the delete request must include the x-amz-bypass-governance-retention:true header (Option D). Together these satisfy governance-mode bypass, allowing the unneeded data to be removed.

Option A is incorrect because increasing the Retain Until Date extends protection rather than removing it, which prevents deletion entirely. Options B and E are incorrect because they reference legal hold rather than governance retention; there is no s3:BypassLegalRetention permission or x-amz-bypass-legal-retention header that applies here, and a legal hold cannot be bypassed even by privileged users, so these do not address the governance-mode lock on the objects.

325.text:
Option B is correct because keeping 50% CPU available means targeting roughly 50% utilization, which a target tracking scaling policy set to 50% CPU utilization maintains automatically by adding or removing capacity to hold the target. Layering scheduled scaling policies that scale out before 09:00 and scale in at 17:00 proactively provisions capacity ahead of the predictable daily load increase, combining reactive headroom with proactive provisioning for the known schedule.

Option A is incorrect because a 90% utilization target leaves almost no headroom, contradicting the requirement to keep 50% CPU available for bursts. Option C is incorrect because fixing minimum, maximum, and desired capacity at 2 removes the ability to scale at all and cannot maintain available headroom or handle traffic growth. Option D is incorrect because scheduled scaling alone cannot continuously maintain the 50% CPU headroom requirement; without target tracking it will not respond to actual utilization or traffic bursts.

326.text:
Option A is correct because billing alarms in CloudWatch depend on estimated charge metrics that are published only after the management/payer account enables Receive Billing Alerts in billing preferences. In AWS Organizations, consolidated billing flows to the payer account, so if billing alerts are not turned on there, the EstimatedCharges metrics are not available and member account managers cannot create billing alarms even with correct IAM permissions.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Resource Access Manager shares resources such as subnets and Transit Gateways, not billing information, and is not involved in enabling billing alarms. Option C is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service and has no bearing on the ability to configure billing alarms. Option D is incorrect because AWS Config evaluates resource configuration compliance and does not monitor or enable billing metrics required for billing alarms.

327.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CLI calls to AWS services are authenticated using SigV4 signing that relies on an access key ID and secret access key. If no access key is configured, the CLI cannot sign the request and authentication fails. Configuring valid credentials, for example through aws configure or an IAM role, resolves the inability to authenticate the call.

Option A is incorrect because the IAM console password is used for Management Console sign-in, not for programmatic CLI authentication, which uses access keys. Option B is incorrect because a missing server certificate would relate to TLS or endpoint trust issues, not to authenticating the caller's identity. Option C is incorrect because an SSH key pair is used to connect to EC2 instances over SSH and has no role in signing AWS CLI API requests, so it cannot be the cause of the authentication failure.

328.text:
Option B is correct because sharing an encrypted RDS snapshot across accounts requires sharing both the snapshot and access to the AWS KMS key that encrypted it. You must use a customer managed KMS key, update its key policy to grant the target accounts permission to use the key, and then share the snapshot with those accounts. This keeps the data encrypted at rest end to end while enabling the other accounts to copy and restore it.

Option A is incorrect because downloading and decrypting the snapshot is not a supported operation for RDS snapshots and would defeat the encryption-at-rest requirement. Option C is incorrect because building an EC2 instance and manually capturing EBS snapshots is convoluted, does not preserve the RDS snapshot workflow, and adds unnecessary steps. Option D is incorrect because creating an unencrypted instance and exporting database contents to a file violates the requirement that all data remain encrypted at rest.

329.text:
Option D is correct because IAM Access Analyzer can generate a least-privilege policy based on the access activity recorded in AWS CloudTrail. After CloudTrail logging is enabled, Access Analyzer reviews the actions the role actually performed over a period and produces a policy template scoped to only those permissions, allowing the administrator to replace the overly permissive policy with one tailored to the application's real usage.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Security Hub aggregates security findings and does not generate IAM policies from CloudTrail activity. Option B is incorrect because EventBridge is not the data source for policy generation; Access Analyzer relies on CloudTrail logs, not EventBridge events. Option C is incorrect because get-generated-policy retrieves the results of a policy generation that was already started from CloudTrail data; on its own it does not enable CloudTrail or initiate the analysis, so it does not by itself meet the requirement.

330.text:
Option A is correct because a CloudWatch Logs metric filter continuously scans incoming log events for a defined pattern and emits a CloudWatch metric, so it can count occurrences of the HTTP 404 response automatically as logs arrive. Because the log events share a consistent structure, the filter pattern reliably matches 404 responses and increments a metric that can drive dashboards and alarms, making it the most operationally efficient monitoring solution.

Option B is incorrect because a subscription filter streams matching log events to a destination such as Lambda, Kinesis, or OpenSearch for further processing; it does not itself produce a count metric. Options C and D are incorrect because running Logs Insights queries on a schedule via Lambda or a custom script requires building and maintaining query and scheduling logic, only samples a fixed past window such as one hour, and is far more operationally intensive than a continuously evaluated metric filter.

331.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL controls SSL enforcement through a database parameter. By creating a custom DB parameter group and setting rds.force_ssl to 1, the SysOps administrator forces all client connections to use SSL/TLS, rejecting any unencrypted connection attempts. This is the supported, in-database way to require encryption for all connections to the instance.

Option A is incorrect because a security group rule controls network access by IP and port; it cannot detect or require SSL encryption on a connection. Option B is incorrect because a KMS encryption key encrypts data at rest on the storage volume, which does not enforce encryption of data in transit between clients and the database. Option D is incorrect because RDS is a managed service and does not support installing custom PostgreSQL extensions to patch in SSL/TLS; SSL support is provided by the managed engine and enforced through parameters.

332.text:
Option B is correct because a Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint, combined with a forwarding rule, lets resources in the VPC forward DNS queries for the on-premises domain to the on-premises DNS server over the Site-to-Site VPN connection. Configuring the outbound endpoint to conditionally forward queries for the internal domain resolves the name resolution errors so the migrated application can locate and connect to the on-premises customer data system.

Option A is incorrect because deploying and maintaining custom DNS forwarder EC2 instances is far more operationally complex than the managed Resolver outbound endpoint and is unnecessary. Option C is incorrect because Direct Connect connections and a link aggregation group address network connectivity and bandwidth, not DNS forwarding, and you cannot repoint the VPC's built-in resolver address to an on-premises server. Option D is incorrect because a public hosted zone serves internet-facing records and network ACLs do not forward DNS queries, so this would not resolve the internal on-premises domain.

333.text:
Option A is correct because the Application Load Balancer publishes a RequestCount metric to CloudWatch that captures the total number of requests it processed. By charting RequestCount over the 2-week period with a 1-minute period, the SysOps administrator can directly see total request volume per minute and identify exactly when traffic exceeded the 100-requests-per-second threshold, using existing metrics with no custom instrumentation.

Option B is incorrect because EC2 instances do not natively emit per-request count metrics, so metric math summing instance metrics would not produce an accurate total request count and is unnecessary when the ALB already provides RequestCount. Option C is incorrect because launch configurations define instance launch parameters and cannot create aggregated request metrics. Option D is incorrect because EventBridge matches discrete events such as state changes rather than counting HTTP requests, so it cannot build a meaningful total request metric for this analysis.

334.text:
Option B is correct because deploying an Amazon RDS read replica offloads read traffic, such as the reporting job's queries, from the primary instance. Pointing the reporting job at the read replica's reader endpoint reduces the read load and Read IOPS on the primary, improving performance, and read replicas can also be promoted, which improves overall availability. This directly addresses both the performance and availability requirements.

Option A is incorrect because while ElastiCache can cache results, it requires application changes and a caching strategy and does not by itself improve the RDS instance's availability. Option C is incorrect because CloudFront is a content delivery network for HTTP content and cannot serve as a caching layer in front of a relational database. Option D is incorrect because simply increasing the instance size may raise raw capacity but is a costly, blunt approach that does not separate read workloads or improve availability the way a read replica does.

335.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Storage Gateway gateway-stored volumes keep the entire dataset on premises as the primary copy while asynchronously backing it up to AWS as EBS snapshots. The volumes are presented as iSCSI block devices that are POSIX-compatible block storage, satisfying the requirement that the backup application write to block-based, POSIX-compatible storage while keeping all data available locally with low latency.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage, not block-based POSIX-compatible storage, so the backup application cannot write to it as required. Option B is incorrect because S3 Glacier is archival object storage with retrieval delays and is likewise not block-based or local. Option C is incorrect because gateway-cached volumes keep the primary dataset in Amazon S3 and retain only frequently accessed data in a local cache, so the full dataset is not available locally, which violates the requirement that all data be available locally.

336.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Artifact is the self-service portal for downloading AWS compliance reports and audit artifacts, including the PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance and related documentation for the AWS-managed infrastructure. The SysOps administrator can retrieve the applicable PCI reports directly from AWS Artifact and provide them to the auditors, which is the intended and authoritative source for AWS infrastructure compliance evidence.

Option B is incorrect because CloudTrail log files record API activity in the company's account and do not constitute AWS infrastructure compliance documentation. Option C is incorrect because CloudWatch logs capture operational and application telemetry, not third-party-validated compliance attestations for the AWS infrastructure. Option D is incorrect because granting auditors administrative access to the production account is a serious security risk and still would not demonstrate compliance of the underlying AWS-managed infrastructure, which is evidenced through AWS Artifact reports.

337.text:
Options A and E are correct because cache hit ratio depends on how often CloudFront can serve an object from its edge cache rather than forwarding to the origin. Forwarding only the cookies, query strings, and headers that the application actually needs (option A) prevents CloudFront from creating a separate cache key for every minor variation, which fragments the cache and lowers hits; minimizing these values consolidates requests onto fewer cache entries. Increasing the TTL settings (option E) keeps objects in the edge cache longer before they expire and must be re-fetched from the origin, directly raising the probability that a subsequent request is served from cache.

Option B is incorrect because changing the Viewer Protocol Policy to HTTPS only enforces secure transport but has no effect on how objects are cached or matched. Option C is incorrect because presigned cookies and URLs are an access-restriction mechanism for private content and do not improve cache efficiency. Option D is incorrect because automatic compression reduces transferred bytes and improves transfer performance, but it does not increase the number of requests served from the cache rather than the origin.

338.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Backup is a fully managed, centralized backup service that integrates with AWS Organizations to apply backup policies across all member accounts from the management account. Using cross-account backup policies, the SysOps administrator can define a single backup plan that automatically protects all EC2 instances organization-wide, with no per-account scripting or custom infrastructure to maintain, making it the most operationally efficient approach.

Option A is incorrect because deploying and maintaining a Lambda function in every account is custom, self-managed code that adds significant operational overhead and duplicates functionality AWS Backup provides natively. Option B is incorrect because a CloudFormation stack set that only adds an AutoBackup tag does not actually perform any backups; tagging alone creates no snapshots. Option D is incorrect because a service control policy is a guardrail that restricts permissions across accounts; SCPs cannot run snapshots or perform any scheduled action, so they cannot create backups.

339.text:
Option D is correct because performing an online resizing (scale up/down) lets you change the node type from extra-large to large in place while the ElastiCache for Redis cluster stays available, preserving the Multi-AZ replication across two Availability Zones and maintaining high availability. Since the cluster shows 75% freeable memory, the nodes are oversized, so moving to a smaller node type reduces cost while keeping the same node count and failover capability with no manual data migration.

Option A is incorrect because decreasing from two nodes to one node removes the replica in the second Availability Zone, eliminating Multi-AZ failover and violating the high-availability requirement. Options B and C are incorrect because building a new cluster and migrating or restoring data from a backup is operationally heavy and introduces downtime or a cutover window; an online resize achieves the same smaller node type far more simply and without the risk and effort of a full migration.

340.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Glacier Vault Lock lets you enforce compliance controls such as a write-once-read-many (WORM) policy that no account can later modify. You attach the vault lock policy in an in-progress state and then must complete the lock by validating it with the returned lock ID within 24 hours; once locked, the policy is immutable and prevents any modification of the archived data, satisfying the regulatory requirement.

Option A is incorrect because the validation must occur within the 24-hour window, not after it; if you wait past 24 hours the in-progress lock expires and the policy is not committed. Options C and D are incorrect because the requirement is to archive data on S3 Glacier with an immutable vault policy, and while S3 Object Lock provides WORM protection, governance mode allows users with special permissions to override or remove the retention, so it does not prevent modification by any account; compliance mode would be needed, making the governance-mode options unsuitable.

341.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon S3 Cross-Region Replication automatically replicates object metadata, along with the object data itself and tags, to the destination bucket when objects are uploaded after replication is enabled. This metadata, including content type and user-defined metadata, is carried over by default so the replicated copy matches the source object.

Option A is incorrect because S3 CRR does not replicate objects for which the bucket owner does not have permissions; the replication role must be able to read the objects, and ownership and ACL constraints prevent replication of such objects by default. Option B is incorrect because objects already stored in the S3 Glacier storage classes are not replicated by CRR. Option C is incorrect because replication only applies to new objects created after replication is configured; pre-existing objects are not replicated by default and require S3 Batch Replication to copy them.

342.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EBS snapshots support the Recycle Bin feature, which uses retention rules to retain deleted snapshots for a specified period instead of permanently removing them. With a Recycle Bin retention rule in place, an accidentally deleted snapshot can be recovered at any time within the retention window, directly meeting the requirement to recover snapshots for a specified period after deletion.

Option A is incorrect because EBS snapshots do not have a deletion protection toggle like the requirement implies, and even a protection setting would block deletion rather than enable recovery after deletion. Option B is incorrect because an IAM deny policy based on snapshot age would prevent deletion but would also block legitimate cleanup and still would not recover a snapshot already deleted; it does not provide a recovery window. Option D is incorrect because a custom Lambda function copying snapshots to S3 Glacier is unnecessary operational overhead and is not how EBS snapshots are archived; Recycle Bin is the purpose-built, managed solution.

343.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Config provides a managed rule, such as the access-keys-rotated or iam-user-unused-credentials-check rule, that automatically identifies IAM users whose credentials have been unused for a defined number of days. Pairing this with an AWS Systems Manager Automation runbook as the remediation action lets the noncompliant finding trigger automatic disabling of the access keys with no custom servers to manage, making it the most operationally efficient approach.

Option A is incorrect because building a Step Functions workflow plus a scheduled EventBridge rule and Lambda to detect inactivity is unnecessary custom orchestration when a managed Config rule already detects unused credentials. Option B is incorrect because running a weekly batch process on an EC2 instance introduces a self-managed server that must be patched and maintained, adding operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because it not only relies on a self-managed EC2 script but also deletes IAM users entirely, which exceeds the requirement to disable keys and passwords and is destructive.

344.text:
Option D is correct because to encrypt traffic without forcing existing users to change their endpoint, you keep the same www.example.com hostname and add encryption transparently. Modifying the existing HTTP port 80 listener to redirect to HTTPS on port 443 ensures clients that still connect over HTTP are automatically upgraded to a secure connection, while the new HTTPS listener on port 443 uses the validated ACM certificate to terminate TLS and forward traffic to the target group. This delivers end-to-end encryption between the client and the ALB seamlessly.

Option A is incorrect because it adds an HTTPS listener but leaves the HTTP listener serving unencrypted traffic, so requests on port 80 remain unencrypted. Option B is incorrect because deleting the HTTP listener would break existing users who connect over HTTP, since their requests would no longer be answered or redirected. Option C is incorrect because an HTTP listener cannot present an SSL/TLS certificate or perform encryption; SSL certificates are only specified on HTTPS listeners.

345.text:
Option B is correct because Session Manager relies on the SSM Agent communicating with the Systems Manager service, which requires the EC2 instance to have an instance profile granting it permission to register as a managed instance. Attaching the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore managed policy to the instance profile provides exactly those permissions. Because the other instances in the same subnet work and the users have Session Manager permissions, the missing piece is the instance-side role, so adding this policy lets the failing Ubuntu instance become a managed instance and accept Session Manager connections.

Option A is incorrect because Session Manager does not use inbound port 22; the agent makes outbound calls to Systems Manager, so opening SSH would be unnecessary and contrary to the security policy. Option C is incorrect because Session Manager connections do not depend on configuring the agent with a specific OS user name to function. Option D is incorrect because Session Manager does not use SSH key pairs at all; it establishes sessions through the SSM Agent and the Systems Manager API, not via private keys.

346.text:
Option C is correct because RDS Proxy is a fully managed database proxy that pools and reuses database connections, preventing the application from exhausting the MySQL connection limit that causes the too many connections error. The only change required is pointing the application configuration at the RDS Proxy endpoint, which keeps code changes to a minimum, and it works with the existing Multi-AZ instance without resizing, making it the most cost-effective fix.

Option A is incorrect because moving to a larger instance size increases cost and only modestly raises the connection limit, treating the symptom rather than efficiently managing connections. Option B is incorrect because migrating from RDS for MySQL to DynamoDB is a NoSQL platform change requiring extensive application rewrites, which is the opposite of minimal code changes. Option D is incorrect because a memory-optimized instance class is more expensive and, like option A, only scales connection capacity rather than pooling connections, so it does not address the problem cost-effectively.

347.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Macie is a fully managed data security service that uses machine learning and pattern matching to discover, classify, and identify sensitive personal information such as names, credit card numbers, and other PII in Amazon S3. Creating a discovery job that uses Macie's managed data identifiers scans the S3 objects and reports the sensitive data found, directly meeting the requirement to classify data and find sensitive personal information.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Config evaluates resource configuration compliance and cannot inspect the contents of S3 objects to detect sensitive personal information. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Rekognition analyzes images and video for objects and faces, not text-based personal information in arbitrary files, so it is the wrong tool for content classification. Option C is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty S3 protection detects suspicious access and threat activity against S3, but it does not classify file contents or identify personal information within the data.

348.text:
Option D is correct because an EC2 instance store provides ephemeral block storage physically attached to the host, delivering the highest possible I/O performance with very low latency. Since the workload needs only a temporary cache that changes frequently and does not need to survive instance restarts, the volatile nature of instance store, where data is lost on stop or termination, is acceptable and the performance advantage makes it ideal for a transient cache.

Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they are all network-attached Amazon EBS volumes, which inherently have higher latency than directly attached instance store. General Purpose SSD gp3 (option A) and Provisioned IOPS SSD io2 (option B) are durable and persistent, offering features the workload does not need at the expense of raw latency, while Throughput Optimized HDD st1 (option C) is a magnetic, throughput-oriented volume with poor random I/O and the lowest performance for a frequently changing cache.

349.text:
Option B is correct because a CloudWatch alarm can monitor the EC2 CPUUtilization metric and trigger a built-in EC2 stop action when utilization stays below a threshold for a sustained period. Configuring the alarm to stop the instances when average CPU utilization is below 5% for 30 minutes reliably detects idle development instances and stops them automatically, directly meeting the requirement with a native, low-overhead alarm action.

Option A is incorrect because the absence of EC2 API activity in CloudTrail does not indicate that a running workload is idle; instances can be busy with no API calls, so this would misjudge usage. Option C is incorrect because VolumeReadBytes measures disk read activity rather than overall instance usage, and CloudWatch metrics themselves do not stop instances; an alarm with a defined action is required. Option D is incorrect because AWS Config reacts to configuration changes, not to whether an instance is in use, so it cannot determine idleness or appropriately stop instances when they are not being used.
